research.lens: Data journalism and explainer media: how respected outlets present model/benchmark comparisons and "state of AI" stories to general, non-expert readers, with a focus on making contested numbers and methodology legible.

patterns.name: Problem-First Entry, Not Methodology-First Entry
patterns.description: Open the list page (and each detail page) with a concrete, relatable finding rather than a definition of what a benchmark is. The Pudding and Bloomberg graphics both anchor complex data in a single striking fact or human-scale example before layering the analytical frame. For benchmarks: lead with a surprise ('Models now score above 90% on MMLU, which used to seem hard. That is why nobody cites MMLU anymore.') rather than starting with 'MMLU is a multiple-choice test of 57 subjects.' The finding earns the reader's attention; the methodology explanation is the reward for staying.
patterns.whyItWorksForUs: Non-technical students skip explanations of mechanics they did not ask for. Starting with a saturation story or a contested-number story hooks them before the taxonomy arrives. This also immediately signals that the page is editorial and opinionated, not a raw data dump, which fits the TCO club's voice.

patterns.name: Consistent Anatomy Per Entry (Parallel Slot Structure)
patterns.description: Every benchmark entry on the list and detail pages should fill identical named slots in the same order: (1) what it measures in one sentence, (2) how scoring works in one sentence, (3) who runs it, (4) current top-3 with score plus date, (5) watch out (one or two named concerns). The ngrok AI-benchmarks article and the evidentlyai.com '25 benchmarks' piece both demonstrate this: readers who develop a template in their head for the first entry can scan all subsequent entries at a fraction of the cognitive cost. Slots that are empty or contested should still appear, filled with an explicit 'not available' or 'disputed' state rather than being silently omitted.
patterns.whyItWorksForUs: The design system already encodes this logic through SectionEyebrow labels and HairRule separators on the editorial surface. Parallel slot structure is a direct mapping from the existing visual grammar to the data shape. It also makes the v1 static build trivially extensible: adding a new benchmark means filling the same template, not designing a new layout.

patterns.name: Inline Freshness and Confidence Signals (Not Just a Footer Date)
patterns.description: Arena.ai's leaderboard shows scores as '1510 +/- 11' directly next to the number, making uncertainty part of the primary data display rather than a footnote. Responsible data journalism practice (documented in Open Notebook, CJR, and multiple vis-research papers) calls for annotating the number itself, not just adding a disclaimer paragraph below it. Concretely: show 'as of [Month Year]' as a secondary line immediately below or beside each score, styled at --text-xs in --color-text-2. For contested numbers, add a small inline chip or symbol (e.g., a circle-i glyph from Phosphor) that expands or links to the caveat. The status 'self-reported', 'contested', 'saturated', or 'fast-moving' should be machine-readable (a data attribute) and visually scannable (a soft tinted label chip).
patterns.whyItWorksForUs: AISA Atlas must never mislead a student into thinking a score is current and uncontested when it is not. The design system has the --color-gold-soft / --color-gold amber pair, the --color-accent-soft blue pair, and a --color-incorrect-dim red pair, all of which can distinguish 'dated', 'contested', and 'deprecated' states without adding new color tokens. This is a data-journalism pattern that directly addresses the product's spec requirement: 'this number is contested or dated must be legible, not just shown in a clean leaderboard.'

patterns.name: Stratified Reliability Tiers, Visible in the List View
patterns.description: The Medium '2026 benchmarks what the numbers mean' piece and the ngrok explainer both implicitly stratify benchmarks by how trustworthy their numbers are. The list surface should make this explicit with a second-order status badge per benchmark (alongside or below the category tag): something like 'Active and live', 'Saturated (effectively solved)', 'Contested methodology', or 'Deprecated'. FiveThirtyEight historically used similar confidence classifications on forecasts. This lets a student scan the list and immediately see that HumanEval is saturated, SWE-bench Verified is contested, and LiveCodeBench is the most contamination-resistant, without reading every detail page. These tiers map to Phosphor icons and the existing tile-color system.
patterns.whyItWorksForUs: The Pulse Index (trends) already groups by category tab. The benchmarks index needs a different orthogonal axis: trust status. Students from non-technical backgrounds are not able to intuit that 90% on one benchmark is meaningless while 25% on another is remarkable; a visible tier label collapses that context requirement into a scannable glyph.

patterns.name: Example Task as Anchor (Show, Do Not Only Tell)
patterns.description: The Pudding's guide emphasizes that abstract statistics carry a fraction of the persuasive weight of concrete examples. Evidently AI's benchmark article and the ngrok piece both include actual sample questions or task descriptions alongside the definition. Each detail page should include one verbatim or paraphrased example task or question (clearly labeled 'example task'). For GPQA Diamond: one of the 'Google-proof' biology questions that even PhDs find hard. For SWE-bench: 'Fix the bug in this real Python library that a developer filed as a GitHub issue.' This gives a student a felt sense of difficulty that no accuracy percentage alone can convey.
patterns.whyItWorksForUs: This is especially important for benchmarks like GPQA Diamond, HLE, and FrontierMath where the whole point is that the difficulty is viscerally surprising. A student who reads '74% expert accuracy on GPQA Diamond' understands something. A student who also reads one of the questions understands why that number is impressive. This fits the editorial surface voice: magazine-style illustration of the concept rather than dry table data.

patterns.name: Separated 'What to Watch' Section (Caveats as Content, Not Disclaimer)
patterns.description: Responsible data journalism (Open Notebook panel on COVID data, CJR's Curious Journalist's Guide, multiple vis-research papers on uncertainty) shows that caveats buried in footnotes are effectively invisible to most readers. The right pattern is to give the caveat content its own slot with its own visual weight, roughly equal to the main data. The Epoch AI site does this for the FrontierMath funding controversy: a separate blog post linked from the benchmark page, not a parenthetical. For /benchmarks detail pages, a named section 'What to watch' (matching the benchmark-research.json field) should be typographically parallel to 'How scoring works', not subordinated as fine print. The ThinBar accent primitive can mark this section in amber (--color-gold) to distinguish it from neutral descriptive content.
patterns.whyItWorksForUs: The product explicitly requires that contamination/saturation caveats be legible. Elevating caveats to first-class content sections rather than footnotes is both a design-journalism best practice and a direct fulfillment of that spec constraint. It also reinforces TCO's credibility with students: the organization is willing to say 'this number is not trustworthy' rather than just listing impressive scores.

patterns.name: Two-Level Navigation: Index Scan + Detail Drill
patterns.description: Bloomberg and Our World in Data consistently separate the 'overview layer' (list of things with summary data) from the 'detail layer' (full methodology, historical context, caveats). The list view shows only the name, one-line description, category tag, trust-status chip, and the top-1 leader with a 'see all' affordance. The detail page holds everything else. This progressive disclosure pattern keeps the index scannable (like the Pulse Index two-up grid) while making the detail pages genuinely deep reference. The Epoch Capabilities Index does the inverse (combines everything on one page) and the result is that non-experts bail before reaching the caveats.
patterns.whyItWorksForUs: The Pulse Index already proves this pattern works for AISA Atlas. Benchmarks is a larger and more numeric surface but the same principle applies. The index row should be readable in under 8 seconds; the detail page rewards a student who spends 3-5 minutes. This also keeps v1 static builds clean: the index is a flat list with minimal data, and each detail page is a richer markdown-or-MDX document.

patterns.name: Cross-Link to Concept Catalog as the Educational Payoff
patterns.description: Our World in Data consistently ends each data explainer with links to related topics. The Pudding ends visual essays with 'go deeper' sections. For /benchmarks, each detail page should end with a clearly labeled cross-link cluster to the related concept(s) in the AISA Atlas catalog (e.g., 'To understand why GPQA Diamond is hard, read: Reasoning' and 'Prompt Engineering'). This is the editorial handoff: the benchmark page creates the need to know; the catalog page satisfies it. The 'editorial-link' primitive with the arrow nudge is exactly right for this.
patterns.whyItWorksForUs: This pattern closes the literacy loop that is TCO's actual goal. Without the cross-link, a student learns that GPQA Diamond measures graduate-level reasoning but has no path to understanding what reasoning even means. The link is the product's value proposition made visible.

references.name: ngrok: 'What those AI benchmark numbers mean'
references.why: Strong structural pattern: tabbed navigation per benchmark, identical slot order (what it tests, methodology, scores, caveats, author judgment), and the critical rhetorical opening that signals scores require interpretation before showing any number. Borrow the 'maybe' framing device and the dedicated Critiques section per benchmark.

references.name: Evidently AI: '25 AI Benchmarks'
references.why: Best example of category-grouped list structure with parallel anatomy (paper link, dataset link, leaderboard link consistently per entry). The 'Limitations of AI benchmarks' section as a standalone block separate from the individual entries is the right model for handling contamination and saturation at the index level rather than only on detail pages.

references.name: Epoch AI: Capabilities Index (ECI)
references.why: Borrow the FAQ-driven caveat pattern: explicit Q&A sections that address cherry-picking, benchmark gaming, and saturation directly, rather than hiding concerns. Also note the useful technique of calibrating a scale using named familiar models as reference points rather than raw absolute numbers.

references.name: Arena.ai (LMArena): Text Leaderboard
references.why: The '1510 +/- 11' score display format is the gold standard for inline uncertainty. Borrow the confidence-interval display directly adjacent to the score. Also note the changelog blog pattern for communicating when methodology changes, which /benchmarks can adopt for version-stamped leader updates.

references.name: Epoch AI: FrontierMath and the OpenAI funding disclosure
references.why: Model for elevating a controversy to a named, linked resource rather than burying it in a footnote. The benchmark data page links out to the disclosure post. /benchmarks should use the same pattern: contested benchmarks carry a visible 'controversy' link, not a parenthetical disclaimer.

references.name: The Pudding: Visual Storytelling Process
references.why: The core principle: anchor abstract statistics in a specific individual data point or concrete example before presenting the aggregate. For /benchmarks this means showing one example task or question per benchmark to give the difficulty a felt sense before reporting accuracy percentages.

references.name: Artificial Analysis: LLM Leaderboard
references.why: Instructive counter-example. The site buries temporal context (72-hour rolling window) in the FAQ rather than showing it adjacent to the data. Use this as a negative reference: /benchmarks should never make a reader hunt for the 'as of' date.

references.name: Medium: 'Every Major AI Benchmark in 2026 - What the Numbers Actually Mean'
references.why: Best example of the 'comparative absence' technique: showing what a lab published alongside what it chose not to publish (e.g., Llama 4's GPQA score vs. its ARC-AGI-2 score of 0%). /benchmarks can borrow this editorial stance: a 'notable omissions' note on benchmarks where leading labs have declined to publish scores.

pitfalls: Publishing scores without inline dates. In a field where numbers move every few weeks, an undated score on a static page looks authoritative but may be 6-18 months stale by the time a student reads it. Every score must carry a visible month/year label, styled as secondary text immediately adjacent to the number, not relegated to a page footer.

Clean-leaderboard aesthetics that erase signal about data quality. Showing a ranked table with pristine numbers and no visual differentiation between 'verified by independent test', 'self-reported by the lab', and 'derived from a contaminated subset' teaches students that all numbers are equally trustworthy. The contested/self-reported status must be a first-class visual element, not a footnote.

Jargon-defined-by-jargon in the 'what it measures' slot. Writing 'GPQA measures graduate-level scientific reasoning' is meaningless to a student who does not know what graduate-level scientific reasoning means in an LLM context. The one-line description must use the benchmark's own performance contrast as the definition: 'GPQA asks questions that domain-expert PhDs answer correctly only 65% of the time, even with internet access.'

Assuming score magnitudes are self-evident. 75% on ARC-AGI-2 and 75% on MMLU mean completely different things. Without a calibration anchor ('random chance on MMLU is 25%; experts score around 89%; 75% used to be impressive but is now below the frontier') a student cannot interpret the number. Every score needs at least one reference point: human baseline, chance baseline, or prior SOTA.

Conflating list-level browse with detail-level learning. Overloading the index row with caveats, methodology detail, and full leader tables makes the list unscannable. The index row should carry only: name, one-line description, trust-status chip, and the single top model with score and date. Everything else lives on the detail page.

Treating 'saturation' as a negative without explaining why it matters. Telling students 'MMLU is saturated' without explaining that saturation means 'models now score so high that differences between them are within measurement noise, so the test no longer tells us which model is better' turns a useful insight into meaningless jargon.

Omitting the 'why a normal person should care' line. Data journalism for general audiences consistently fails when it answers 'what' without answering 'so what.' Every benchmark detail page needs a plain-English answer to 'why does this test's result matter for how AI will affect my life or field' before the methodology section. Placing this line at the top, not the bottom, prevents students from bailing before reaching the payoff.

Using heatmaps or color gradients to rank scores across benchmarks. Scores from different benchmarks are not comparable (75% on HLE is superhuman; 75% on MMLU is below-frontier). A rainbow gradient across benchmark rows implies comparability that does not exist and will mislead non-expert readers.
research.audienceFit: University students from mixed, often non-technical backgrounds are the exact audience that data journalism (The Pudding, Bloomberg graphics, Our World in Data) optimizes for: people who are curious, somewhat skeptical of institutions, and willing to engage with complexity if the entry cost is low and the first sentence rewards them. The benchmarks surface maps well to this lens because the core editorial insight (labs use benchmarks to claim progress; most benchmarks are either gamed, saturated, or contested; the honest picture is more interesting than the leaderboard) is itself a good story, not just a data table. The pitfall is that this audience will quickly detect condescension (over-explaining) or false confidence (clean numbers with no uncertainty). The right register is a TCO club member who has done the reading and is now giving a fellow student a straight account of what they found, including the parts that are messy. That is exactly the editorial voice the existing Pulse Index and This Week digest already use, making the surface a natural extension of the established pattern rather than a new register to invent.

research.lens: Leaderboards and ranked score displays: how live ranking products handle numeric data, temporal freshness, uncertainty, and contested numbers for a non-expert audience.

patterns.name: Inline confidence interval as uncertainty signal
patterns.description: Arena.ai shows Elo scores as "1510 plus or minus 11" directly in the rank column, not buried in footnotes. The plus-or-minus suffix is typeset small and muted, so the headline number reads first but the uncertainty is never hidden. Papers With Code uses asterisks on entries where measurement data is incomplete. Artificial Analysis uses empty dashes for unavailable metrics rather than zeroes or blanks that imply a real measurement.
patterns.whyItWorksForUs: Our benchmarks carry hard-to-explain uncertainty (overlapping confidence intervals on Arena Elo, contested FrontierMath scores, near-saturated MMLU-Pro gaps). Showing the interval inline, right next to the number, makes "this ranking is not definitive" legible to a student without requiring them to read a methodology page. A small parenthetical like "(plus or minus 11)" or a tinted badge reading "near tie" can carry this idea with zero technical vocabulary.

patterns.name: Dated snapshot badge anchored to each leader panel
patterns.description: The dominant failure mode across existing leaderboards is stale numbers with no provenance. The best mitigation, used in financial and sports dashboards rather than AI leaderboards, is a compact "as of [date]" label anchored visually to the data it governs, not in a global footer. Sofascore and NBA.com tie timestamps directly to score cells in live standings. Artificial Analysis surfaces a global "metrics are based on the past 72 hours" statement at page top. The editorial solution is a per-panel stamp: a 11px uppercase label reading "LEADERS AS OF JUN 10 2026" sitting on a hairline rule immediately above the top-3 list.
patterns.whyItWorksForUs: v1 is static with manually set numbers. Students who trust a number from a screenshot or a class slide will never see it update. A visible dated stamp makes the staleness visible by design, not by accident, and sets the expectation that a human must re-check. The existing TrendsClient already uses a 36-hour stale banner for the same reason. The benchmarks surface should use the same logic per benchmark, not globally.

patterns.name: Soft numbered badge as rank, name as hero
patterns.description: The Pulse Index (trends-client.tsx) already does this: a category-tinted number badge sits left, the trend name is the typographic hero at 18-20px weight 600, and metadata lines sit below at 11-13px muted weight. Arena.ai uses position number plus trophy glyph for the top three. Papers With Code uses a rank column with a highlight on the first-place row. The pattern is consistent: the ordinal rank is a compact visual anchor, not the content itself. The name of the entity (model, paper, trend) does the heavy lifting.
patterns.whyItWorksForUs: For /benchmarks the list surface shows benchmark names as heroes, not models. For the detail-page leader panel, the pattern inverts: the rank badge (1, 2, 3) is the anchor, the model name is the hero, and the score sits below as supporting metadata. This matches how a student would say it aloud: "number one is Claude something, scored 75.7%". The existing badge pattern from TrendsClient can be reused with minimal variation.

patterns.name: Contested or provisional flag as inline annotation, not tooltip
patterns.description: Artificial Analysis uses an asterisk next to provisional scores. Financial data displays use a "P" badge (provisional) or a "(est.)" suffix inline. Wikipedia's citation-needed inline flags are a non-technical precedent everyone has seen. The key principle is that the flag appears in the reading flow, not on hover. Tooltips hide information behind interaction that many mobile users and casual readers never trigger.
patterns.whyItWorksForUs: Several of our benchmarks have genuinely contested data: FrontierMath's self-reported o3 score with funding conflicts, HLE's answer-error rate, Arena Elo's statistical ties at the top, and SWE-bench Verified's uncertain status after reported deprecation. An inline flag, a small tinted chip reading "disputed" or "self-reported" in 11px, makes the editorial judgment visible on the first pass. This is exactly what a teacher would circle in red on a printout. Non-experts need the flag to be obvious, not optional.

patterns.name: Saturation state as a categorical label, not a score
patterns.description: Papers With Code has a concept of benchmark saturation (performance plateauing near human-level) but does not surface it visually. A better pattern comes from sports: a "clinched" or "eliminated" label on a standings row signals a categorical state, not a numerical position. For benchmarks, a small label reading "Effectively solved" or "Near ceiling" on the list surface is more honest and more useful than showing a raw score of 92% with no context.
patterns.whyItWorksForUs: MMLU-Pro was created because original MMLU saturated, with top models clustering at 88-92%. HumanEval is widely considered saturated. Showing a percentage with no saturation context misleads non-experts into thinking the gap is meaningful. A categorical label next to the score ("near ceiling," "active," "unsolved") lets students understand at a glance whether the benchmark still discriminates. The label can double as a filter on the list view.

patterns.name: Hairline-separated top-3 leader panel with vertical rule accent
patterns.description: The editorial design system already has a 2-column split with a 1px vertical rule (used in digest-client and the TwinColumn pattern). For a leader panel, this translates to: rank badge on the left of a vertical hairline, model name plus score plus lab plus date on the right. Three such rows, separated by horizontal hairlines, make a compact top-3 block that reads like a race result without needing a full sortable table.
patterns.whyItWorksForUs: A full sortable table is overkill for v1, which ships with only 3 leaders per benchmark. The 2-column hairline split earns a distinct visual treatment from the main list while staying inside the no-card, no-box constraint. It also degrades cleanly: when a weekly cron eventually refreshes the leader lines, only the data changes, not the component structure.

patterns.name: Score as absolute number plus plain-English interpretive gloss
patterns.description: Every existing leaderboard presents raw percentages or Elo integers with no translation. Research on numeracy and risk communication (Gigerenzer, Kahneman) consistently shows non-experts misread percentages without a reference point. Sports broadcasts always pair a score with a record or standing: "72 wins, first in the West". A benchmark leader entry can do the same: "87.5% (human experts: 81%)" or "Elo 1510 (top of 360+ models)" supplies the comparison point in the same visual element.
patterns.whyItWorksForUs: Non-technical students will not know whether 75% on GPQA Diamond is impressive or mediocre. A one-line interpretive gloss in muted 12px below the score, such as "human PhD experts scored 81%", gives the number meaning without requiring the student to go elsewhere. This is the primary gap all current AI leaderboards have: they are built for practitioners who already know the scale.

references.name: Arena.ai leaderboard (arena.ai/leaderboard)
references.why: Borrow the inline plus-or-minus confidence interval display directly next to each score, and the categorical tabs (Text, Coding, Vision) for filtering. Avoid: no visible per-entry timestamps, no explanation of what Elo means for a newcomer, and no staleness indicator.

references.name: Artificial Analysis LLM Leaderboard (artificialanalysis.ai/leaderboards/models)
references.why: Borrow the asterisk-notation for provisional scores and the empty-dash convention for missing data. Also borrow the global freshness statement ("metrics based on past 72 hours"). Avoid: the heavy sortable table with expandable columns is too dense for a non-expert literacy surface.

references.name: Papers With Code SOTA tables (paperswithcode.com)
references.why: Borrow the per-row date column showing when each result was submitted, which makes the passage of time legible in the table itself. Also borrow the idea of linking each row to its source paper. Avoid: no saturation labels, no plain-English context, sorted only by score with no qualitative tier groupings.

references.name: Humanity's Last Exam leaderboard (labs.scale.com/leaderboard/humanitys_last_exam)
references.why: Borrow the Rank (Upper Bound) metric which counts how many models are statistically significantly better, a clean way to show uncertainty without requiring students to parse a confidence interval. The concept translates to a simple label: "statistically tied with ranks 2 and 3".

references.name: LiveCodeBench leaderboard (livecodebench.github.io/leaderboard.html)
references.why: Borrow the difficulty-tier breakdown (Easy, Medium, Hard) as a way to show that a single overall score conceals variation. For our detail pages, showing that a model scores differently on different difficulty bands is more informative than one number.

references.name: NBA.com standings (nba.com/standings)
references.why: Borrow the categorical state labels ("clinched playoff spot", "eliminated") as a model for our saturation labels. Also borrow the convention that the timestamp of the standings is shown at the top of the page, not in a footer, because the audience knows standings go stale fast.

pitfalls: Raw percentages without a reference point mislead non-experts. A score of 75% sounds like a B-minus on a test, not a frontier result. Always pair a number with its human baseline or a plain-language scale anchor.

Showing no timestamp is the most common failure across all existing AI leaderboards. For a static v1 surface this is especially dangerous: a student who finds the page 6 months later will have no idea the numbers are old. Every leader panel must carry an explicit as-of date.
Tooltips as the sole delivery mechanism for uncertainty information are invisible on mobile and ignored by casual readers. Contested or provisional status must appear inline, not on hover.

A sortable table of 10 or more columns is the wrong pattern for a literacy surface. The audience has not opted in to dense data analysis. The top-3 leader panel should be a simple ranked list, not a table, and the full benchmark list should be a compact index not a spreadsheet.

Treating all benchmark scores as equivalent hides the method differences. An Elo score from pairwise crowd votes (Arena) is a completely different thing from a pass-rate on fixed questions (SWE-bench). The list surface needs a visible tag or label showing the score type so students do not compare apples to integers.

Removing caveats to make the surface look cleaner backfires for an education product. The entire point of this page is to teach students why benchmark claims are complicated. Omitting the "disputed" or "self-reported" flag on FrontierMath or the "near tie" flag on Arena Elo teaches the wrong lesson.

Using red or green color alone to signal good or bad scores assumes the student knows the direction of the scale. Some benchmarks score higher-is-better (MMLU-Pro percentage), some are harder to interpret (Elo has no ceiling). Color should reinforce a label, never replace it.

Founding the UI on a clean top-1 leader per benchmark creates false precision. For Arena Elo, ranks 1 through 3 are within each other's confidence intervals as of June 2026. The design must be able to represent a genuine tie or a near-tie without picking a false winner.
research.audienceFit: University students from mixed, mostly non-technical backgrounds are the target. This audience has high familiarity with ranked lists (sports, music charts, college rankings) and low familiarity with statistical uncertainty or benchmark methodology. The strongest design moves are those that borrow the visual grammar of sports standings (rank badge, dated snapshot, categorical state labels, score plus record gloss) while stripping out the practitioner affordances (sortable columns, raw Elo math, confidence interval notation). The single biggest lift this surface can provide over existing AI leaderboard products is the interpretive gloss: a one-line plain-English translation of what a score means relative to human performance. Every other leaderboard skips this because its audience already knows. This audience does not, and that is the editorial opportunity.

research.lens: Editorial literacy reference: how "what it measures / why it matters / how it works / watch out for" structures work for contested, numeric, technical topics aimed at newcomers

patterns.name: Anchored Plain-Sentence Definition
patterns.description: Every entry opens with one sentence that defines the benchmark without using any jargon that is not immediately unpacked. Our World in Data does this at the top of every indicator page: a single bolded sentence stating what the metric counts, not what it implies. Wikipedia leads each article with a first sentence that can stand alone. The sentence must pass a bus-stop test: a person who has never read an AI paper should be able to repeat it accurately after one reading.
patterns.whyItWorksForUs: Our audience is explicitly mixed-background. If the definition section uses words like 'distributional shift' or 'pass@1' without a gloss, the reader exits. A single plain sentence also anchors the rest of the detail page: every subsequent section can say 'remember, this benchmark measures X' and the reader has a hook. For benchmarks like GPQA Diamond and HLE where the name itself signals nothing, this pattern is load-bearing.

patterns.name: Layered Disclosure (Overview First, Then Depth)
patterns.description: Stripe's developer docs and the best Wikipedia articles gate-keep complexity behind progressive disclosure. The overview section contains: what, why-it-matters-in-one-sentence, and a key number. The detail below it contains: how scoring works, who runs it, where to find the leaderboard. The criticism/watch-out section comes last, after the reader has understood what the benchmark is trying to do. MDN Web Docs applies this same pattern for API references: the signature, then the description, then the exceptions and gotchas. The structure is roughly: Setup (what it is), Stakes (why care), Mechanics (how scored), Skepticism (watch out for).
patterns.whyItWorksForUs: A student who only reads the first two sections should leave with something true and useful. A student who reads all four sections should leave skeptical in a productive way. This matches the data shape we already have (one-line what, why-it-matters, scoring, criticisms). It also lets the 'contested number' callout live in section four without ambushing the reader before they know what the benchmark does. For our specific dataset, SWE-bench Verified and FrontierMath have non-trivial criticisms that need context before they land.

patterns.name: Skepticism as a Named Section, Not a Footnote
patterns.description: Our World in Data labels a section 'Data Quality and Definitions' on every indicator. The Stanford AI Index Report separates 'Limitations' into a visible callout box, not a footnote. The Financial Times data journalism team uses an inline 'What this chart cannot tell you' label directly beneath contested visualizations. The pattern: skepticism is given structural parity with the positive description, not buried. On detail pages for contested benchmarks, the watch-out section is not an asterisk but a first-class heading.
patterns.whyItWorksForUs: Our data has real contested claims: FrontierMath's OpenAI funding controversy, HLE's disputed answer-error rate, SWE-bench Verified's possible deprecation, Arena scores as near-statistical ties. If these appear as footnotes, students will not register them and will quote the clean leaderboard number as fact. A named section called 'Watch out for' or 'Known criticisms' at the same visual weight as 'How scoring works' trains the reader to expect complexity in benchmark claims. This is the core literacy goal of the surface.

patterns.name: Dated Number + Source Attribution Inline
patterns.description: The Our World in Data chart footer shows 'Source: World Bank (2023)' beneath every figure. The Economist's data journalism practice is to label any number that may age badly with the as-of date inline in the body text, not just in a citation list. Wirecutter's recommendation pages show 'last tested March 2024' prominently near the verdict. The pattern: the number and its date-of-observation appear together in the same visual unit, not separated by a citation key.
patterns.whyItWorksForUs: Arena Elo scores change within days. ARC-AGI-1 scores from December 2024 are stale. Our data already carries as-of dates per score. The visual treatment must make the date part of the number, not a subscript the reader skips. For our design system, this means the leader panel should show '87.5% (Dec 2024, ARC Prize)' as one unit, with the parenthetical in a smaller weight but still inline, not in a hover tooltip or footer note. Students who screenshot the leader panel should capture the date automatically.

patterns.name: Contested Status as a Visual Signal, Not Just Copy
patterns.description: The Wikipedia 'disputed' and 'needs citation' inline tags are orange banners that make the status machine-readable to the eye before the reader processes the text. The Fitch Ratings watch list uses a yellow 'Rating Watch Negative' badge next to the score rather than in the prose. The Metaculus forecast site shows a confidence interval band alongside any estimate, visually communicating uncertainty without requiring the reader to understand the concept first. The pattern: uncertainty has a shape (badge, band, color) as well as a sentence.
patterns.whyItWorksForUs: For our editorial system with its no-card, typography-led approach, the signal can be extremely minimal: a small 'CONTESTED' or 'DATED' eyebrow label in the SectionEyebrow style (11px uppercase), or a ThinBar rendered in a muted amber instead of the default accent blue to indicate a caveat score. This avoids adding card boxes or colored alert blocks, which would break the editorial surface aesthetic. The visual signal means a student who skims the leader panel still absorbs the caveat before reaching a conclusion.

patterns.name: Cross-Link as Reading Path, Not Navigation Utility
patterns.description: The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy links every technical term in the opening section to its dedicated entry, with anchor text that is the exact term, not 'click here' or 'learn more'. The Khan Academy sidebar shows prerequisite concepts with a completion state, framing them as a reading order. The Stripe docs pattern: every concept that is needed to understand a more advanced page links back to the simpler concept at the point of first use. The editorial-link primitive in our system (underline sweep plus arrow nudge) already supports this interaction pattern.
patterns.whyItWorksForUs: Each benchmark entry specifies 2-4 related AI concepts a student should understand (reasoning, agents, multimodality, code generation, etc.). Rather than listing these at the bottom as a 'related concepts' list, the cross-link pattern embeds them at the exact sentence where the concept is first needed. When the GPQA description says 'domain-expert questions in biology, physics, and chemistry', the word 'domain-expert' links to the catalog concept for knowledge depth vs. breadth. This turns the detail page into a reading path toward the catalog rather than an isolated reference entry.

patterns.name: The Two-Column Explainer Split
patterns.description: The Stripe docs layout for complex concepts uses a two-column split: left column is the editorial prose explanation, right column is the actual artifact (a code sample, a schema diagram, a real number). Wikipedia's infobox is the same idea: structured factual data lives at a fixed position while prose lives beside it. The Guardian's data features split a map or chart on the right from the explanatory text on the left, so the two layers can be read together or independently. Our existing design system already has a 2-column split with a 1px vertical rule as a primitive.
patterns.whyItWorksForUs: For the benchmark detail page, the natural split is: left column = the editorial prose (what it measures, why it matters, how scoring works, watch out for), right column = the leader panel (top-3 scores with dates and sources). This keeps the contested numbers visible alongside the explanatory prose that contextualizes them, so a reader who finds the right column first immediately sees that the left column provides the skepticism layer. The vertical rule hairline is already a design-system primitive, making this zero-cost to implement.

patterns.name: Saturation Status as a Category Label
patterns.description: Consumer Reports uses 'Best Buy' and 'Recommended' category labels for products, and 'Under Test' for pending reviews. Wirecutter shows 'Still The Best' vs 'New Pick' as a named category, not a sentence. The Red Hat documentation team uses status labels like 'Technology Preview' and 'Deprecated' as visible badges, not prose caveats. The pattern: a benchmark's evaluation status (actively discriminating vs. saturated vs. contested) is a property of the entry, visible at list-row level, not only revealed on the detail page.
patterns.whyItWorksForUs: Our list surface shows ~13 benchmarks in rows. A student scanning the list needs to know at a glance whether MMLU-Pro is still live and discriminating or whether it is approaching the saturation that killed original MMLU. A small status label on the list row (e.g. 'ACTIVE', 'NEARING SATURATION', 'CONTESTED') gives structural parity between entries that are healthy and entries that need skepticism, without requiring the student to read every detail page to find this out. This is especially important for HumanEval, which most students will have heard of and may incorrectly assume is current.

references.name: Our World in Data (ourworldindata.org)
references.why: The gold standard for layered disclosure on contested numeric data for non-expert audiences. Each indicator page gives a plain-sentence definition at the top, a named 'Data Quality and Definitions' section, inline source attribution with the year next to every number, and cross-links to related indicators at the point of first use, not just in a related-articles sidebar. Borrow the 'Data source: [Name], [Year]' inline pattern directly for the leader panel, and the section structure (Overview, How it works, Data quality) for the detail page scaffold.

references.name: Stripe Developer Docs (stripe.com/docs)
references.why: Best example of the layered disclosure + two-column split applied to complex technical concepts for people who are not experts in the underlying domain. The left-prose / right-artifact split is exactly what the benchmark detail page needs (left: editorial explanation; right: live leader panel). Borrow the progressive-disclosure structure: one-sentence summary at the top, mechanics in the middle, caveats and edge cases at the end. Also borrow the 'Related guides' inline cross-link pattern for the catalog concept links.

references.name: Wikipedia lead-section and infobox pattern
references.why: The infobox (right column, structured key-value data) alongside prose (left column, editorial explanation) is the ur-example of the two-column explainer split applied to reference content. The lead section policy is strict: the first sentence must define the subject without jargon. Borrow the structure: infobox position for the leader panel, lead-section discipline for the opening definition sentence, and the 'Criticism' section heading pattern for the watch-out section.

references.name: Metaculus forecast pages (metaculus.com)
references.why: Shows how contested or fast-moving numeric estimates can be presented with visual uncertainty (confidence bands, forecast ranges, last-updated timestamps) without requiring the reader to understand the underlying statistics. The 'Community Prediction: X% (last updated today)' pattern is a direct analog for 'Leader: 87.5% (Dec 2024, ARC Prize)'. Borrow the inline timestamp-plus-source unit and the visual treatment of uncertainty as a range, not a point.

references.name: Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy (plato.stanford.edu)
references.why: Best example of dense technical content made navigable for non-specialists through aggressive cross-linking at the point of first use (not in a bibliography at the bottom). Every technical term in the opening section links to its dedicated entry. The result is that the reader can go as deep as they want without the prose being dumbed down. Borrow the pattern of linking to catalog concepts at the exact sentence where the concept first appears, not in a 'See Also' list.

references.name: The Economist data journalism style (economist.com)
references.why: Their practice of inline as-of dates for numbers that age ('as of April 2024') and 'What this chart cannot tell you' explanatory notes directly beneath contested visualizations. The pattern of making the date part of the number (not a footnote) is precisely what our leader panel needs. Also borrow the editorial tradition of naming the source inline in the body text rather than relegating it to a citation list that readers skip.

pitfalls: Showing clean leaderboard numbers without inline date-plus-source attribution. Students will screenshot a clean top-3 table and share it as current fact. The date and source must be part of the same visual unit as the score, not a footnote.

Burying the 'Watch out for' section after the leader panel. Students who reach the leader panel often stop reading. The skepticism layer must appear before or alongside the scores, not after them. For highly contested benchmarks (FrontierMath, HLE, Arena Elo), a brief caveat should appear even on the list row, not only on the detail page.

Using relative freshness language ('recently', 'as of this writing') instead of explicit dates. The benchmark data moves fast. Relative dates become misinformation within weeks. Every score needs a calendar date.

Treating saturation as a footnote. MMLU being effectively saturated is not a minor caveat, it is the reason MMLU-Pro exists. If saturation is not given structural prominence, students will cite saturated benchmarks as evidence of frontier capability. Consider a visible status label on the list row.

Using jargon in the plain-sentence definition. Defining GPQA Diamond as 'a subset of GPQA that evaluates expert-level factual recall on multi-hop reasoning tasks' fails the bus-stop test. Avoid: pass@k, n-shot prompting, calibration, distributional shift in opening sentences. These can appear in the mechanics section with a gloss.

Conflating the full benchmark with its subset. The research data explicitly flags this: GPQA Diamond has 198 questions and different expert accuracy than the full 448-question GPQA. SWE-bench Verified (500 instances) is not the same as SWE-bench (2,294 instances). The plain-sentence definition must name the specific variant being described.

Presenting Arena Elo as a stable ranking. The top-3 overlap within confidence intervals, and the board can shift within two days of a model release. The leader panel for arena.ai must visually signal that this is a live ranking with overlapping uncertainty bands, not a definitive ordered list.

Making contested scores look contested only through copy. A sentence saying 'this score is disputed' is weaker than a visual signal (a muted amber ThinBar instead of the accent blue, or a CONTESTED eyebrow label) that communicates status before the reader processes the text. Our editorial system needs a lightweight visual vocabulary for caveat states without adding card boxes or colored alert blocks.

Listing related concepts at the bottom as a 'See Also' section. Students rarely read footers. Cross-links to catalog concepts should appear inline at the sentence where the concept is first needed, using the existing editorial-link primitive.
research.audienceFit: TCO students are university-level but explicitly mixed-background, meaning the surface must do two jobs simultaneously: it must not condescend to students who have some CS background, and it must not lose students who have none. The best reference surfaces that achieve this (Our World in Data, Stripe docs, Stanford Encyclopedia) share a structure where the first 150 words are plain enough for a non-expert and the last section is detailed enough for a practitioner. For this specific topic, the non-expert concern is not complexity but false confidence: students who see a clean leaderboard number will quote it as fact without knowing it may be dated, self-reported, or statistically tied with the model below it. The literacy goal of this surface is productive skepticism, not just knowledge transfer. Every design pattern should serve that goal: the plain definition teaches what the benchmark measures, the dated score teaches that numbers age, the named skepticism section teaches that numbers are contested, and the cross-links teach that understanding a benchmark requires understanding the concepts it claims to measure. A student who finishes a detail page should leave able to say not just 'GPT-X scored Y on MMLU-Pro' but 'MMLU-Pro measures broad academic knowledge using 10-option questions, GPT-X scored Y as of [date], and the benchmark may itself be approaching saturation because frontier models are converging around Z'."

research.lens: Curated Index / Award Editorial: how a tasteful curated list reads (Typewolf, Awwwards, magazine contents pages, "best of" indexes). Focus on the LIST surface rhythm: numbered badges, a hero name, restrained metadata, and crisp rules.

patterns.name: Soft Numbered Badge as Anchor
patterns.description: A small, rounded or lightly tinted badge showing the ordinal (01, 02, 03...) sits flush-left at the start of each row. On Typewolf's favorite-sites index the number is the visual anchor before the site name; on Awwwards' Sites of the Day list the sequence badge creates a sense of curated rank without being a hard ranking. The badge uses a pastel tint from the palette, not the full accent color, so it recedes behind the name. Typeset in mono or tabular figures so column alignment holds across all row heights.
patterns.whyItWorksForUs: Benchmarks are not a ranked leaderboard in the traditional sense, some are academic, some are crowdsourced, and the numbers just mark "here is entry 7" rather than implying #7 is worse than #6. The soft tint (e.g., --tile-indigo-bg with --tile-indigo-fg) communicates position without implying a league table. Hanken Grotesk's tabular figure support handles alignment. The badge also doubles as a visual counting aid for a non-expert who wants to know at a glance that this is a real, bounded list, not an infinite feed.

patterns.name: Name as the Hero, No Subtitle Clutter
patterns.description: On Typewolf's list pages the website name is the largest typographic element in the row, set at roughly 18-22px with tight tracking. Everything else (foundry, fonts used, date) runs at 11-12px below or beside it. The name carries all the visual weight; secondary metadata is optically quiet. No box, no card border, no background: the typography alone creates the hierarchy. This is the same pattern Awwwards uses on its Sites of the Day index and what magazine contents pages do with story titles versus page numbers.
patterns.whyItWorksForUs: Benchmark names like 'GPQA Diamond', 'SWE-bench Verified', or 'Humanity's Last Exam' have inherent character. At 18-20px Hanken Grotesk semibold on the warm #FAF9F5 ground they become sticky and memorable. Students landing on /benchmarks for the first time do not need another leaderboard table; they need the name to land first so they can associate it with the plain-English description below. The existing --text-xl (24px) or --text-md (16px) token handles this without new scale additions.

patterns.name: The One-Line Deck: Plain English First
patterns.description: After the name, every curated index gives one short plain-language descriptor, not a marketing subtitle. Fonts In Use writes "Typeface used for the wordmark and logotype" on each entry. Magazine contents pages write a single line of standFirst copy. Typewolf's monthly roundups write one clause: what the site does and what makes it notable typographically. The deck line is set at 13-14px in text-2 (--color-text-2, #6B6B73) and never exceeds one line on desktop.
patterns.whyItWorksForUs: The benchmarks-research.json data already provides a 'what it measures in one plain sentence' per benchmark. That sentence IS the deck line. A student seeing 'Tests whether a model can fix real bugs in real Python codebases' at 13px below 'SWE-bench Verified' at 20px immediately understands the content tier without clicking. The opacity difference (#1F1F22 vs #6B6B73) is the only visual device needed, which matches the existing editorial surface pattern.

patterns.name: Crisp 1px Hairline as the Sole Separator
patterns.description: Typewolf's index uses one rule between rows, not cards or alternating backgrounds. Awwwards' editorial lists use a similar bottom-border-only row separator. The rule color is barely perceptible on light backgrounds, roughly equivalent to the existing --color-border (#E8E5DC). This technique is the same as the existing HairRule primitive and the 1px rules already separating the two-column trend grid in TrendsClient. Rows breathe with 24-32px vertical padding on each side of the rule.
patterns.whyItWorksForUs: Adding boxes or cards around benchmark rows would signal 'these are independent products to purchase' rather than 'this is an index of a field you are learning about.' The hairline keeps the surface feeling like a magazine index or a reading list, not a marketplace. It is also the cheapest implementation: border-top: 1px solid var(--color-border) on each row, matching the existing trend-grid pattern exactly.

patterns.name: Restrained Metadata Strip: Category Tag, Score Hint, and a Freshness Signal
patterns.description: After the deck line, a small metadata strip runs at 11px uppercase spaced (matching the existing SectionEyebrow pattern). Typewolf includes 'FONTS USED' and 'TAGS' in this slot. Awwwards includes category and score badges. The key discipline: never more than three metadata atoms in this strip, each separated by a centered dot or thin space, not a dash or pipe. For a contested number, this strip is where a 'NUMBERS DISPUTED' or 'SATURATED' tag sits, set in a muted amber tile color rather than the accent blue.
patterns.whyItWorksForUs: Each benchmark has a domain category (coding, reasoning, math, multimodal, human preference) and a status (live, contested, saturated). The research data flags specific entries: SWE-bench Verified has an OpenAI deprecation note, HLE has a known answer-error rate, FrontierMath has a funding-access controversy, ARC-AGI-2 numbers are still moving. The strip handles all of this without cluttering the hero name or the deck line. 'CONTESTED' in amber tile, 'SATURATED' in slate tile, domain category in the appropriate pastel tile creates a scannable status system that students can read without understanding the detailed caveats below.

patterns.name: Scannable Two-Column Split at Desktop Width
patterns.description: Typewolf's 50 Best Sites and Awwwards' gallery grids both use a two-up layout at wider viewports: two entries per visual row, separated by a 1px vertical rule down the center. This is exactly the 'two-column split with a 1px vertical rule' primitive already described in the design system and already implemented in TrendsClient's trend-grid class. At 720px and below it collapses to a single column.
patterns.whyItWorksForUs: With 8-14 benchmarks in v1, a single-column list would require too much scrolling to scan the full set. A two-up layout lets a student see the whole index in one viewport height on a laptop. The existing trend-grid CSS class (.trend-grid in globals.css with gridTemplateColumns: 'repeat(2, 1fr)') is directly reusable. No new layout primitive needed.

patterns.name: The Freshness / Caveat Flag as a First-Class Design Element
patterns.description: Award editorial sites signal when a winner's status is in question: Awwwards uses a 'Honorable Mention' label in a different color to distinguish it from a full SOTD. Magazine 'best of' lists print a dagger (obelus) and a footnote when a product has since changed. The pattern is a small colored flag token (not a footnote) inline in the metadata strip. The flag is visible in the list view so the student knows before clicking that the entry needs a caveat. On the detail page, the caveat is spelled out in full plain English under a 'Watch Out For' section heading.
patterns.whyItWorksForUs: This is the hardest design problem for /benchmarks: the data is contested and the spec explicitly says 'the design MUST make this number is contested or dated legible, not just show a clean leaderboard.' Award editorial solves this by making the status a visual citizen of the row, not a footnote hidden below the fold. A student who sees 'CONTESTED' or 'DATED NUMBERS' in a muted amber badge before clicking has been warned at the right moment. This is more honest and more legible than a disclaimer paragraph at the top of the page.

patterns.name: Hover State: Underline Sweep on the Name Link Only
patterns.description: Typewolf and Awwwards both animate only the title text on row hover, not the whole card. The editorial-link pattern (an underline that sweeps in from left on hover, with a subtle arrow nudge) matches this exactly. Nothing else in the row changes on hover: no background fill, no shadow, no scaling. This is the existing editorial-link primitive.
patterns.whyItWorksForUs: Students using a mouse get clear affordance that the benchmark name is a link to the detail page, without the row feeling like a button or a shopping card. It is also the cheapest and most accessible interaction: no JavaScript, just CSS. The arrow nudge on the name confirms directionality (you are going somewhere) without needing an explicit arrow icon cluttering the row.

references.name: Typewolf: 50 Best Sites / Favorite Sites index
references.why: The canonical reference for the numbered-badge plus hero-name plus metadata-strip row pattern at 1px rule separators. Directly borrow: the optical sizing relationship between the ordinal badge (small, tinted), the site name (hero), and the font/tag metadata (11px uppercase). Also borrow the restraint: no card chrome, no hover background, just type and air.

references.name: Awwwards: Sites of the Day and Editorial Layout collection
references.why: Borrow the status badge pattern: Awwwards distinguishes SOTD, Honorable Mention, and Developer Award with small colored labels in the row, not a separate page. This is the direct precedent for the 'CONTESTED' and 'SATURATED' flag tokens in the metadata strip. Also useful: how Awwwards editorial layouts use typography scale alone (no boxes) to separate entries at high density.

references.name: Fonts In Use: main index
references.why: Fonts In Use is the closest analog to what /benchmarks needs to be: a serious, dense, searchable index of a technical field presented for a generalist curious audience. Borrow the structure of each entry: a name, a one-line use description, a category tag, and a contained image slot (which on /benchmarks becomes the leader mini-panel). The site earns credibility through restraint, not decoration.

references.name: Awwwards: Editorial Layout inspiration collection
references.why: A live browsable set of sites that use editorial hierarchy without card chrome. Useful for confirming that typography-only separation (weight, size, color-text-2 opacity) is legible at the density a benchmark index requires. Also a reference for how the 'freshness date' metadata line is handled at small scale without breaking visual flow.

references.name: It's Nice That: The Nice List and feature indexes
references.why: It's Nice That's curated list format uses a numbered item title with a short plain-English line beneath it and almost no chrome. The page breathes more than Typewolf and is closer in feeling to an editorial magazine contents page. Borrow: how it groups entries into loose categories with a SectionEyebrow-style label, which maps directly to the benchmark domain categories (Coding, Reasoning, Math, Multimodal, Human Preference).

pitfalls: Turning the list into a leaderboard table. A table with score columns implies precision, comparability, and currency that the data does not support. Students will treat the numbers as authoritative. Use the table only inside the detail page, behind a click, where context and caveats are visible.

Hiding caveats in a footnote or disclaimer paragraph. If 'CONTESTED' or 'DATED NUMBERS' only appears on the detail page after a click, a student who scans the list gets a false impression of clean data. The caveat flag must live in the metadata strip of the list row itself.
Using em dashes or en dashes anywhere in copy (hard constraint from the project brief and memory). Use commas, periods, parentheses, or a plain hyphen for score ranges like 70-75%.

Overloading the metadata strip. More than three atoms (category tag, status flag, 'as of' date) in the 11px strip turns it into unreadable noise for a non-expert. Pick the two most important and cut the rest to the detail page.

Animating the whole row on hover. A background-fill hover on the full row turns the entry into a card and implies it is a product or a shopping item, not an entry in a reference index. Only animate the hero name link (underline sweep).

Making the numbered badge visually heavy. If the ordinal badge uses the full --color-accent (#4255FF) at full opacity, it fights the benchmark name for attention. The badge should recede behind the name. Use the pastel tile palette (e.g., --tile-indigo-bg / --tile-indigo-fg) at a small size (roughly 20x20px) so the eye lands on the name first.

Stacking more than one line of deck copy in the list view. Two lines of descriptor is the limit before the row becomes a mini-article and loses the index rhythm. One crisp sentence is the target. Save the second sentence for the detail page.

Implying the leader-panel numbers are live when v1 is static. The 'as of [date]' timestamp must appear inline in the leader panel, not just on a 'last updated' page footer. Students will share screenshots; the date must travel with the number.

Using score percentages without a denominator or context. '75% on GPQA Diamond' means nothing to a student who does not know that PhD-level experts score 81% on the same set. The metadata strip or a hover tooltip needs to anchor the number with a reference point: 'expert baseline: ~81%'.

Collapsing domain categories that are conceptually different. 'Math' (AIME, FrontierMath) and 'Science reasoning' (GPQA Diamond) feel similar to a student but measure different things. A domain tag that conflates them will confuse rather than orient. Keep category tags specific and brief.
research.audienceFit: University students from mixed, often non-technical backgrounds need two things from this surface that the curated-index lens provides directly. First, a sense of bounded scope: seeing 12 numbered entries in a clean list communicates 'this is a knowable, complete set of things, not an infinite feed.' That lowers the perceived cost of learning the space. Second, clear entry points: a hero name at reading size plus one plain-English sentence lets a student decide in under two seconds whether this entry is relevant to something they already heard about (e.g., 'I read that Claude beat GPT on a coding test, where is that?'). The lens breaks down if any part of the row requires prior knowledge to interpret: score numbers without a reference baseline, acronym-only names without a deck line, or category tags that assume familiarity with ML research conventions. The curated-index pattern works for this audience because it was originally designed for generalist curious readers, not domain experts: Typewolf is aimed at designers who want to appreciate typography without being typographers, and Fonts In Use is aimed at people who want to understand how type works in the real world. The same stance, applied to benchmarks, treats the student as curious and capable without requiring them to already be an AI researcher.

proposal.persona: The Editorial Minimalist. I extend the existing Pulse Index / home / digest language with maximum restraint. Type, opacity, and hairlines do all the work. The number is the quietest thing on the page, never the loudest. My priority is cohesion: a student who knows /trends and /digest should feel /benchmarks is the same publication, one more section of the same magazine, not a new product with a leaderboard bolted on.
proposal.designLanguageName: The Standings. A reading-room standings page, not a leaderboard. The word "standings" is honest about ranked numeric data while signaling, like sports standings, that the order is provisional, dated, and goes stale, which is exactly the literacy lesson. It is the Pulse Index grown one degree more sober for contested numbers.
proposal.bigIdea: Treat a benchmark score the way a careful editor treats a quote: never print it without its date and its source, and never let a clean number imply more certainty than it has. The whole surface is built so that "this number is contested or dated" is structurally impossible to miss, achieved with zero new color tokens and zero card chrome, purely through the existing editorial grammar. The core move is demotion of the number: across the entire site the hero is always a name (trend name, headline, concept), so here too the benchmark name is the hero and the score is quiet metadata. A list row never shows a score at all, only a one-word status word (Active, Saturated, Contested, Dated) that already encodes whether the number can be trusted. The score only appears on the detail page, inside the leader panel, and there it always travels as one inseparable unit: rank, model, score, lab, and "as of MON YEAR" set as a single line so a screenshot cannot strip the date off the number. Contested and self-reported states are not footnotes and not tooltips: they are an amber ThinBar drawn down the left of the leader panel (reusing the gold/amber tier pair, swapped in for the default accent) plus an inline lowercase flag (self-reported, disputed, near tie) sitting in the reading flow right after the score. The editorial voice is a TCO member who did the reading and is giving you a straight account including the messy parts. Every detail page opens problem-first (a saturation or contested story), not definition-first, then runs four parallel named sections in a fixed order, and ends by handing the reader off into the catalog at the exact word where the concept is first needed.
proposal.listSurface: A single-column hairline index at the existing 1080px editorial width, not the two-up grid. I deliberately break from the Pulse Index two-up here: scores are not comparable across benchmarks, and two columns invites the eye to read across rows and compare a 91% next to a 9% as if they were a league table. One column per row forces vertical reading and kills false comparison. Header is the established editorial stack: SectionEyebrow "STATE OF THE MODELS", an H1 "Benchmarks" at clamp(34,4vw,44), one muted deck paragraph in plain English ("The tests labs use to claim progress. Most are gamed, saturated, or contested. Here is the honest picture, with dates."). Below, ghost-text category tabs identical to the Pulse Index tabs (All, Reasoning, Coding, Math, Multimodal, Human preference) with the 2px accent sweep, plus one extra orthogonal filter rendered the same way for trust status (All, Active, Saturated, Contested) so a student can pull up only the shaky ones. Each row is a Link, separated by one 1px hairline (border-top var(--color-border)), 28px vertical padding, no card, no hover fill. Row anatomy, left to right and top to bottom: a soft tile-tinted number badge (the trend-cell badge reused verbatim, 28px circle, tile bg + fg, tabular figures, position only, never implying rank quality) then a category kicker in 11px uppercase tile-fg; the benchmark name as the hero on its own line at var(--text-xl) weight 600 with the editorial-link underline sweep on cell hover; one plain-English deck line under it at 14px text-2, single line, clamped (this is the verbatim "what it measures" sentence from the data); then a quiet 11px uppercase metadata strip with at most three atoms separated by a centered dot: the score TYPE (Accuracy, Elo, Pass rate) so students never compare apples to integers, the trust-status word, and the leader "as of" month. The trust-status word is the only place color enters a row: Active in text-3 (neutral, the default, no alarm), Saturated in slate-fg, Contested and Dated in gold-fg. No score appears anywhere in the list. The status word is the score's stand-in, and it is the honest one.
proposal.detailSurface: Reuses the established two-column editorial split (the trend-detail-split / concept-shell pattern: a flexible main column plus a fixed rail, 1px vertical rule between, collapses to one column under 720px via the existing class hook). Back-link at top (the digest BackLink, arrow nudges left). Header: SectionEyebrow with the category, the benchmark name as H1, and immediately below it the status word rendered larger and in plain language as the standfirst, never as a chip: for a contested one the H1 is followed by a sober line like "The numbers here are contested. Read why before you quote them." This is the problem-first opening the research demands, and it lands before any score. LEFT main column is pure editorial prose in four parallel named sections, each introduced by a SectionEyebrow exactly like the digest's "Why it matters" / "What to watch", in this fixed order: (1) What it measures, opening with one bus-stop-test plain sentence that uses a performance contrast as the definition ("asks questions that PhD experts get right only about 65% of the time, even with the internet open"); (2) Why it matters, the so-what, with a single "example task" pulled out and labeled, set in a slightly indented quieter block so a student feels the difficulty before seeing a percent; (3) How scoring works, naming the metric, the scale, a calibration anchor (chance is 25%, experts ~81%, frontier ~90%), and who runs the board; (4) Watch out for, given full structural parity, its SectionEyebrow rendered in gold-fg instead of text-3 and a 2px gold ThinBar running down its left edge so saturation and contamination read as first-class content, never fine print; this is where the FrontierMath funding controversy or the SWE-bench deprecation gets a full plain-English paragraph and an outbound "read the disclosure" editorial-link. Catalog cross-links are embedded inline at the first sentence where a concept appears (reusing editorial-link on the exact term, "domain-expert reasoning", "code generation"), and a final compact "To go deeper" hairline cluster lists the 2-4 related concepts as editorial-links, closing the literacy loop. RIGHT rail holds the leader panel and nothing else, so the contested-number lesson always sits beside the prose that contextualizes it.
proposal.leaderPanel: A hairline-separated top-3 standings block in the right rail, no table, no sortable columns, degrades to pure data swaps when the weekly cron eventually lands. It opens with a SectionEyebrow-styled stamp on a hairline: "LEADERS AS OF JUN 2026" in 11px uppercase, the date anchored to the data it governs, not in a page footer. If the whole board carries needsRecheck or boardLastUpdated is stale relative to today, the stamp gains a second muted line "board last updated MAR 2026, may be behind" reusing the exact 36h-stale logic the Pulse Index already ships. Three rows, each separated by a 1px hairline, each row a 2-column micro-split mirroring the global vertical-rule primitive: left is the soft rank badge (1, 2, 3 in the tile-indigo circle, tabular), right is a stack: the model name as the hero at var(--text-md) weight 600, then the score at 14px in tabular figures with its plain-English gloss in 12px text-2 immediately beside it on the same line ("94.1% accuracy. PhD experts score about 81%."), then a 12px text-3 line with the lab and the inline "as of JUN 2026". Rank, model, score, lab, and date are one visual unit by construction. The uncertainty and trust signals live inline in the reading flow, never on hover: a lowercase tinted flag follows the score where the data says so (self-reported, disputed, near tie), drawn in gold-fg; for arena Elo I show the interval natively ("Elo 1510 plus or minus 11") and where ranks 1-3 overlap I print "near tie with ranks 2 and 3" rather than fake a clean winner. When any leader in the panel is self-reported or disputed, a 2px gold ThinBar runs down the entire left edge of the panel, the amber-swap of the default accent ThinBar, so a student who only glances at the rail still absorbs the caveat before reading a single number. Empty or unsourced leaders (LiveCodeBench had no trustworthy source) render an explicit "no verified number yet" em-free state, never a blank or a zero that implies a real measurement. Below the three rows, one editorial-link to the official leaderboard, labeled with its real source ("Official board: TIGER-Lab on Hugging Face") so the provenance travels too.

referencePick.name: Our World in Data (indicator page pattern)
referencePick.why: It is the closest match to my persona and to the literacy goal: a plain-sentence definition at the very top, inline 'Source: Name, Year' fused to every number so the date can never be screenshotted away, a named 'Data Quality and Definitions' section given real structural weight rather than a footnote, and cross-links to related indicators placed at the point of first use. That is exactly my four-section detail page, my dated-leader-unit rule, my gold 'Watch out for' parity section, and my inline catalog hand-off, achieved the way I want them achieved: with typographic hierarchy and restraint, not chrome. I am borrowing its discipline (the number and its provenance are one unit; uncertainty is a named section, not a disclaimer) and pairing it with arena.ai's inline 'plus or minus 11' only as the micro-pattern for the one Elo board that needs it.

proposal.repoFit: Reuses almost everything, writes almost no new CSS. Tokens: data-surface="editorial" + data-theme="light" wrappers, --color-bg #FAF9F5 ground, --color-text/-2/-3 for the size+opacity hierarchy, --text-xl for the hero name, --text-md for leader model names, the --tile-*-bg/-fg pastel pairs for number and rank badges, and the gold pair (--color-gold / --color-gold-soft / --tile-gold-fg) as the single caveat color, no new tokens. Primitives lifted verbatim: SectionEyebrow (11px uppercase 0.18em), HairRule, the editorial-link sweep-plus-arrow-nudge (the <style> block in trends-client copied by value), the soft 28px tabular number badge from TrendCell, the ghost-text CategoryTab with the 2px accent sweep (reused for both the category and the new trust-status filter), the digest BackLink, and the digest "Why it matters" eyebrow-over-prose section pattern for all four detail sections. Layout: the right-rail two-column split is the existing concept-shell grid (minmax(0,1fr) + fixed rail, 1px vertical rule, the .trend-detail-split / concept-shell responsive class hooks already in globals.css). The leader ThinBar is the dot-on-track / ThinBar accent primitive recolored from --color-accent to --color-gold. The stale-board stamp reuses the Pulse Index 36h-stale banner logic and the digest stale Banner. New code is data plumbing only: a Benchmark static body plus a BenchmarkLeaderCache shape (already specified in EXPANSION 7.3) feeding components that are recombinations of existing parts. v1 ships static with hardcoded dated leaders; the weekly cron is a pure data swap behind the same components, zero structural change, exactly as the spec wants.
proposal.tradeoffs: Honest costs. (1) I drop the two-up grid that gives the Pulse Index its signature density, so /benchmarks scrolls more for 13 rows and looks a touch more austere than its sibling. I accept this: comparability across non-comparable scores is a real harm here, and single-column kills it; the surfaces stay cohesive through shared primitives, not shared column count. (2) No score in the list view is a strong, opinionated call. A student scanning for "who is winning at coding" must click into a detail page to get a number. I am betting the trust-status word plus score-type tag is the more honest first impression, and that a number without its date and gloss in a scannable row would teach the exact false-confidence lesson this page exists to prevent. If user testing shows students bail without a number, the cheapest additive fix is a single muted leader line per row, dated, behind the status word, not a column of bare percentages. (3) Restraint risks reading as plain. The whole bet of the Editorial Minimalist is that on contested numeric data, plain and sober is the correct register and the differentiator, because every other AI leaderboard is loud and falsely clean. (4) Using gold as the sole caveat color leans hard on one hue to carry contested, self-reported, dated, and saturated; I mitigate by always pairing the color with a literal word (the status word, the inline flag, the named section), never color alone, so it survives colorblindness and grayscale screenshots. (5) The inline-flag and ThinBar system adds per-leader conditional rendering, so the leader component is more branching than a plain table, the price of making uncertainty legible by construction rather than by disclaimer.
