strengths: The data is genuinely ready. Both research passes produced verified, adversarially-checked findings for 12 of 13 benchmarks, and the round-2 JSON already names every trust flag (needsRecheck, selfReported, disputed, near-tie overlaps) that the design needs to surface. The design and the data were developed in tandem, which is rare and is the single biggest risk-reducer for a surface like this.

The single-column list is the correct structural call for non-comparable numeric data. The proposal gives a precise, defensible reason for breaking from the two-up Pulse Index grid: reading across rows would invite false cross-benchmark comparison (91% MMLU-Pro next to 46% HLE looks like a league table). This is not a style preference - it is a data-integrity argument, and it is correct.

Demotion of the number is genuinely novel and pedagogically honest. The choice to show only a status word (Active, Saturated, Contested, Dated) in the list, with no bare percentage, forces the student to arrive at the detail page before seeing a number. Every score in the research data has a provenance problem of some kind, so withholding the number until context is available is the right call for this specific dataset.

The four-section detail structure maps cleanly onto the verified data shape. What it measures, Why it matters, How scoring works, and Watch out for correspond almost exactly to the fields in benchmarks-research-round2.json (whatItMeasures, whyItMatters, scoring, watchOutFor). The fixed order and the gold eyebrow on Watch out for give contamination and saturation structural parity with the definition, which every AI-literacy educator would endorse.

The amber/gold caveat system is disciplined. Using a single hue (--color-gold / --tile-gold-fg) exclusively for trust signals, always paired with a literal word, is a coherent choice that survives grayscale and colorblindness. The inline flag (self-reported, disputed, near tie) carries the meaning even if the color is stripped. This is better practice than icon-only or tooltip-only caveat signals.

The stale-board stamp reuses a mechanism that already exists in the Pulse Index (the 36h-stale trackerStale logic in trends-client.tsx) and directly matches what the research data provides (boardLastUpdated on every leader table). The design exploits a real data field, not a hypothetical one.
risks: The ThinBar primitive does not do what the proposal says it does. In dashboard-client.tsx, ThinBar is a 3px horizontal progress bar (height: 3px, width fills left to right with touchedPct and masteredPct). The proposal treats it as a 2px vertical left-edge rail on the leader panel. These are completely different constructs. Reusing ThinBar verbatim for a vertical left border is impossible. The builder will need a new ~5-line CSS rule or a new variant. This is a small buildability cost but the proposal repeatedly claims zero new CSS, and that claim is false.

The concept-shell grid collapses the aside at 1279px and hides it entirely, not at 720px. The proposal says the detail page two-column split collapses under 720px via the existing class hook. The trend-detail-split class does collapse at 720px and is a valid choice, but concept-shell (which the proposal also names as an alternative) behaves differently and hides the rail completely at tablet widths. The proposal conflates two different grid classes with different breakpoints. A builder picking concept-shell would get the rail disappearing at 1279px, which on a 1080px editorial surface means it never shows.

LiveCodeBench has no verified leader table. The research explicitly flags this: the leaderboard JavaScript is unreadable and the data JSON was unparseable. The proposal lists LiveCodeBench as one of the v1 benchmarks. The leader panel for LiveCodeBench would have to show the explicit no-verified-number-yet state the proposal describes, which is fine in principle, but the proposal does not flag this as a known gap. A builder reading only the design doc would not know the data is missing and might ship a placeholder or silently omit the benchmark.

The two-filter row (category tabs plus trust-status tabs) doubles the filter surface at the top of the list. The Pulse Index has one filter row. Adding a second orthogonal filter axis creates a state-management problem: a student who selects Coding in the category filter and Saturated in the trust filter will likely see zero or one results, since few benchmarks sit at the intersection of both. The proposal does not address the empty-filtered-state case or how the two filters interact. At 13 benchmarks total, the subset after two filters can be a single row, which on a single-column layout with 28px padding looks nearly blank.

FrontierMath carries needsRecheck: true because Epoch AI announced on 2026-05-11 that AI-assisted review flagged fatal errors in roughly one third of the benchmark problems, with corrected scores pending. The proposal does not name this specific situation. The Watch out for section and the gold caveat system would need to explain not just the OpenAI funding controversy but that the benchmark itself may be structurally broken and every listed score is pre-correction. The research data has this, but the design doc does not acknowledge it, so a builder might copy the watch-out text without understanding the severity.

The leader panel design shows three rows for every benchmark, but the research data for several benchmarks has fewer than three distinct verifiable entries, or has tied entries at ranks 1 and 2 (HLE, SWE-bench Verified have tied rank-1 entries). The panel anatomy assumes three clean, ordinal rows. A tie at ranks 1 and 2 would require either showing four rows (both rank-1 entries plus a rank-2 that does not exist) or collapsing to two distinct entries, neither of which the ASCII sketch handles. The proposal mentions near tie in prose but the panel layout is drawn as if ties are one-off edge cases rather than present in the actual top-3 data for at least two of the v1 benchmarks.

The no-score-in-list rule creates a genuine discoverability risk that the proposal acknowledges but underweights. A university student who arrives at /benchmarks from a link saying learn about AI benchmarks and sees thirteen rows with no scores will not know whether this surface is a leaderboard, a glossary, or something else. The status word Active or Saturated means nothing to a first-time visitor without reading the header deck. The proposal bets on the deck paragraph to carry this orientation, but the deck is 22 words and will be skimmed or skipped. The educational intent is correct; the risk is that the student bounces before the lesson lands.

The inline-first stale warning for the leader panel (a second line of muted text under the date stamp, reusing the 36h-stale banner logic) may not be appropriate for a static v1 surface. In v1 the leaders are hardcoded and dated. The stale logic in the Pulse Index measures time since last cron sync. For static hardcoded data there is no syncedAt timestamp to compare against, so the stale banner logic cannot be directly reused. The builder would need either to hardcode a lastUpdated date per benchmark and compare it against Date.now() on the client, or omit the stale signal entirely in v1. The proposal treats static v1 and cron-refreshed v2 as sharing the same component without explaining how the static case populates the timestamp field the staleness check reads from.
critique.verdict: Cohesion is near-perfect and the data-integrity philosophy is exactly right, but three concrete buildability gaps (ThinBar is horizontal not vertical, concept-shell collapses at 1279px not 720px, LiveCodeBench has no leader data) and one real audience risk (no number in the list leaves a first-time visitor with no anchor) need explicit resolution before a builder can execute without guesswork.

proposal.persona: The Data-Forward designer. Students arrive at /benchmarks with one live question: who is winning right now. The score and the top-3 leaders are the hero. My job is to make those numbers instantly scannable and comparable across the whole field without lying about how soft they are. So I let the number be big and proud, then I weld the date, the uncertainty, and the contested flag directly onto it so the number can never travel alone. A clean leaderboard that hides staleness is the failure mode I am designing against, but my answer is not to bury the numbers, it is to armor them.
proposal.designLanguageName: The Standings: a dated leaderboard that wears its caveats on its sleeve
proposal.bigIdea:
Treat /benchmarks like sports standings rendered in the editorial system: rank-led, number-forward, scannable top to bottom, but every score is a welded unit that carries its own date, uncertainty, and trust state so it can never be screenshotted out of context.

The core invention is the ScoreStamp: one inline typographic cluster of (big tabular score) plus (as-of month and year) plus (a confidence or near-tie marker) plus (a one-word trust state). It is the atom that appears identically on the list row (top leader only) and in the detail leader panel (top 3), so a student learns to read one shape once and then reads it everywhere.

Two orthogonal axes do all the work the research demands. A RANK axis: the soft numbered tile badge, reused straight from the Pulse Index, marks list position without implying a hard league table. A TRUST axis: a four-state status word (Active, Near ceiling, Contested, Dated) that sits beside the rank and is allowed to override the bravado of a big number. Rank is never implied by color or by position alone, because Elo ties and saturation make who-is-number-one genuinely ambiguous, so the trust word carries the skepticism in plain English.

v1 is fully static: the as-of date is hardcoded into each ScoreStamp, so a later weekly leader-line cron mutates only the score string and the date string, never the layout or the prose. The component is built for both from day one. This is the data-forward direction inside the no-card editorial family: the number leads, but it is never naked.
proposal.listSurface:
A single-column standings table built entirely from hairlines, not a card grid. I deliberately reject the two-up Pulse Index split here because the list's job is cross-benchmark comparison, and a single column with one shared right-edge ScoreStamp column lets a student's eye run straight down the leaders like a sports table. Width is the standard 1080px content frame.

Header: a SectionEyebrow reading STATE OF THE MODELS, an h1 "Benchmarks", a one-line deck, and one global dated stamp ("Leaders as of June 2026, checked by hand") so the freshness promise is set before the first row. Above the rows, ghost-text filter tabs reused verbatim from CategoryTab, but here they filter the TRUST axis (All, Active, Near ceiling, Contested) not the domain, because trust is the orthogonal axis the Pulse Index does not have. A second quiet row of domain tags (Reasoning, Coding, Math, Multimodal, Human preference) can sort within.

Each row is a 3-column grid: [rank tile badge 28px] [name + deck + meta strip, flex 1] [ScoreStamp, right-aligned, fixed ~240px column]. The right column is the spine: because every row's top-leader ScoreStamp aligns on the same right edge with tabular figures, a student scans 13 scores in one vertical sweep. Left block: the benchmark name as hero at --text-lg/600 (editorial-link sweep on hover), one plain-English deck line at 14px text-2 taken verbatim from the research "what it measures" sentence, then an 11px uppercase meta strip with at most three atoms: domain tag (tile-fg colored) then a centered dot then the trust word. The trust word is the only colored token in the strip: Active in text-3 (quiet, it needs no alarm), Near ceiling in slate-fg, Contested in gold-fg, Dated in gold-fg. Right block (the ScoreStamp): the leading model's score huge in tabular nums at --text-lg/600, the model name at 13px text-2 directly under it, and a 11px uppercase line "MMMM YYYY, source" under that. When the number is a near-tie (arena.ai) the score reads "1510 +/- 11" with the interval in text-3 at 13px inline, and a tiny "TIE" marker replaces a clean rank-1 claim. When Contested, a single Phosphor info glyph in gold sits flush before the as-of line. Rows separate with one 1px var(--color-border) rule and 24px of air top and bottom. No backgrounds, no boxes, no hover fill: only the name link sweeps. A saturated benchmark (HumanEval) shows its score struck-through-quiet at 0.55 opacity with "Near ceiling" so the eye learns instantly that 92% there is not a frontier signal.
proposal.detailSurface:
The detail page is the two-column split made literal: prose left, live numbers right, reusing the .trend-detail-split grid and its 1px vertical rule (.trend-detail-rule) verbatim. The right rail is the data, pinned in view; the left column is the skepticism that contextualizes it. A reader who lands on the numbers first immediately sees the prose is the caveat layer.

Top of page: BackLink ("All benchmarks", reused from digest-client), then the rank tile badge + SectionEyebrow domain kicker, then the benchmark name as a clamp(32,4vw,44) hero (matching the Pulse Index h1), then ONE deck line. Critically, per the research pitfall about jargon-defined-by-jargon, the deck uses the benchmark's own difficulty contrast as the definition: "GPQA Diamond asks questions that PhD-level experts get right only about 81% of the time, even with the internet open." Directly under the hero sits the page-level "Why a normal person should care" line in a single accent-eyebrow block, BEFORE methodology, so a student who bails early still leaves with the payoff.

Left column, in strict layered-disclosure order with HairRule between each and a SectionEyebrow label on each: (1) What it measures, (2) Example task (a real or paraphrased question in a quiet quoted block so difficulty is felt, not just told), (3) How scoring works (metric, scale, who runs it, with the human/chance baseline stated as the calibration anchor: "random guessing scores 25%; experts about 81%"), (4) Watch out for. The Watch out for section is first-class, never a footnote: its SectionEyebrow is the one place I swap the accent to gold, and a 2px ThinBar in --color-gold runs under that eyebrow (the accent ThinBar recolored, the only structural recolor on the page) so the skepticism block is visually unmistakable from the neutral descriptive blocks. For SWE-bench Verified the deprecation note lives here as body text with a "read the controversy" editorial-link, not a parenthetical. The bottom of the left column is the cross-link cluster: "To understand why this is hard, read:" followed by editorial-link concept chips (Reasoning, Code generation) using the exact /concepts/[slug] pattern from digest-client.

Right rail (the hero of the page): SectionEyebrow "LEADERS AS OF JUNE 2026" sitting on a HairRule, then three stacked rows, each a mini 2-column [rank badge | ScoreStamp] separated by hairlines. Row 1 gets the biggest score, but if confidence intervals overlap (arena.ai ranks 1-3) a single "Statistically tied with #2 and #3" line spans under all three rather than faking a clean winner. Each leader row: rank tile badge (1,2,3), model name + lab at 13px, the ScoreStamp score in tabular nums, and the welded "Jun 2026, arena.ai" line. Below the three: the trust word as a labeled status ("STATUS: Contested") and, where the data is a December-2024 anchor not a live number (ARC-AGI-1, FrontierMath baseline), a literal "This is a historical anchor, not a current leader" line in gold so a student is never misled into reading a stale number as current. The rail ends with a quiet "Numbers move weekly. We re-check by hand." note that doubles as the cron's future home.
proposal.leaderPanel:
The live top-3 panel is the ScoreStamp atom stacked three deep inside the right rail, built so the cron later swaps strings and nothing else. Structure: SectionEyebrow "LEADERS AS OF JUN 2026" on a HairRule, then three rows, each a 2-column hairline split [28px rank tile badge | model block]. The model block is the ScoreStamp: model name + lab at 13px/600 text on line one, the score huge in tabular figures (--text-lg/600, the visual hero of the row) on line two, and the welded provenance line "Jun 2026, arena.ai" at 11px uppercase text-3 on line three. The score and its date are one inline unit by construction, so a screenshot always carries the date.

Three states the panel must render honestly, each driven by a data attribute not a tooltip:
1. Clean ranking (MMLU-Pro, GPQA Diamond): three distinct scores, distinct ranks, each with its baseline gloss in 12px text-2 directly under the score ("expert baseline about 81%"). This is the data-forward happy path: number big, context welded.
2. Near-tie (arena.ai Elo): scores render as "1510 +/- 11" with the interval in text-3, and a single spanning line under the three rows reads "Ranks 1 to 3 are within each other's margin: treat this as a tie." No false rank-1 trophy. The badge stays 1/2/3 for reading order but the spanning line removes the bravado.
3. Contested or self-reported (FrontierMath o3 25.2%): a Phosphor info glyph in --color-gold sits inline before the score, the trust word reads "Contested", and a one-line plain-English reason with a "read why" editorial-link sits directly below: "OpenAI funded this benchmark and had access to most problems. Treat this number with caution." For a historical anchor (ARC-AGI-1 Dec 2024) the provenance line itself says "Dec 2024 result, not a current leader" in gold.

Color discipline: I never use a heatmap or green/red gradient across scores, because the research is explicit that 75% on HLE and 75% on MMLU are not comparable. The only colored tokens in the panel are the gold caveat glyph and the gold trust word. Everything else is the neutral editorial palette. The rank badge uses --tile-indigo-bg/fg so it recedes behind the score. v1 hardcodes all of this; the cron's only write surface is the score string, the model string, and the date string per row.

referencePick.name: Humanity's Last Exam leaderboard (Scale, labs.scale.com/leaderboard/humanitys_last_exam) and its Rank (Upper Bound) metric
referencePick.why: It is the one existing AI leaderboard that solves my exact hard problem honestly: instead of faking a clean #1 when scores are statistically tied, it reports how many models are significantly better at 95% confidence, which collapses to a plain-English 'tied with ranks 2 and 3' line. That is precisely the near-tie state my ScoreStamp needs for arena.ai's overlapping Elo top three (1510 +/- 11 over 1504 over 1502). I borrow the upper-bound idea but translate it out of statistics into one editorial sentence a non-technical student reads instantly. I pair it with NBA standings as the mental model for the welded as-of stamp and categorical state words (Active / Near ceiling / Contested / Dated map to clinched / eliminated): an audience that already reads sports tables knows standings go stale and that a label can mean more than a position. I reject Arena.ai's own board as my primary because, while its '1510 +/- 11' inline interval is the gold standard I am literally copying, it buries the as-of date in a FAQ, which is the exact sin my direction exists to fix.

proposal.repoFit:
Reuses, almost nothing new invented. TOKENS: --text-lg (score hero), --text-xl (page hero), --color-text / text-2 / text-3 (the whole hierarchy comes from opacity, no new grays), --color-border / border-subtle (every hairline and the row rules), --color-accent #4255FF (name links, accent eyebrows) via data-surface=editorial, --color-gold + --color-gold-soft (the entire caveat/contested/dated vocabulary, no new alert color needed), --tile-indigo-bg/fg (rank badges), the domain tag colors map onto existing --tile-*-fg pairs exactly like TrendsClient's THEME_TILE map, --radius-2, --space-* scale, and --maxw-content / page padding. fontVariantNumeric tabular-nums (already used by the momentum number and gauge) makes the score column align.

PRIMITIVES copied by value the same way TrendsClient already inlines them: SectionEyebrow (11px uppercase 0.18em), HairRule (from digest-client), editorial-link (the underline-sweep-plus-arrow-nudge, including the exact ::after keyframe block already in trends-client's style tag), BackLink (from digest-client), CategoryTab (the ghost-text 2px-accent-sweep tab, repurposed to filter the trust axis), the numbered tile badge (28px round, tile-bg/tile-fg, tabular-nums, lifted straight from TrendCell), and the ThinBar (recolored to --color-gold for the Watch-out eyebrow).

LAYOUT: the detail page reuses the existing .trend-detail-split grid and its .trend-detail-rule 1px vertical rule plus the <=720px collapse already in globals.css, so the prose-left/leaders-right split is zero-cost. The list is a new but trivial 3-column hairline grid in the same idiom as digest-client's "56px minmax(0,1fr)" article rows. ICONS: the contested glyph is Phosphor "info" via the existing <Icon name="info"> already imported in trends-client. DATA: maps 1:1 onto the spec's Benchmark (static body) + BenchmarkLeaderCache (cron leader line); the ScoreStamp's three swappable strings (score, model, asOf) are exactly the cache's shape. NEW work is only: the ScoreStamp component, the four-word trust enum, and the near-tie spanning line. Everything else is assembly.
proposal.tradeoffs: Honest costs of going data-forward. (1) Single-column list trades the Pulse Index's two-up density for cross-benchmark scannability: with only 13 rows a student scrolls a bit more, but the aligned right-edge score column is the whole point and a two-up split would destroy that vertical read. If the set grows past ~20 I would add domain group headers, not a second column. (2) Leading with the number risks exactly the false-confidence the research warns about; I spend my entire complexity budget on the ScoreStamp's welded date + trust word to neutralize it, which means more authored fields per benchmark (every score needs a baseline gloss and a trust state hand-set in v1). That is real editorial labor, but it is the labor the literacy goal requires. (3) The four-word trust enum is a judgment call I am imposing (calling HumanEval "Near ceiling" or SWE-bench Verified "Contested" is an opinion); I treat that as a feature, the TCO voice taking a stance, but it must be defensible per benchmark and re-checked when leaders refresh. (4) Tabular figures and right-alignment lean slightly more "table-like" than the rest of the editorial family, which is the deliberate tension in my brief: I keep it inside the system by using zero borders, zero fills, and hairlines only, so it reads as standings-in-a-magazine, not a spreadsheet. (5) The near-tie spanning line and historical-anchor line are extra conditional rendering paths that a naive cron could forget to maintain; I mitigate by making the trust state a required enum on the data model so an un-set state fails loud rather than silently showing a clean number. (6) Color-blind safety: trust is carried by the WORD first and gold second, never gold alone, so the contested state survives without color. The one thing I am NOT doing is a comparison heatmap across benchmarks; the research is right that it implies a comparability that does not exist, and I would rather lose the flashy cross-benchmark color than teach a false equivalence.

strengths: Token discipline is genuine: the proposal maps every visual token to a real variable in globals.css (--color-gold, --tile-indigo-bg/fg, --color-border, fontVariantNumeric tabular-nums) and every primitive to an already-instantiated component in trends-client.tsx or digest-client.tsx (SectionEyebrow, HairRule, BackLink, CategoryTab, the numbered badge). The reuse claim is not hand-wavy; it is auditable against the code.

The ScoreStamp atom solves a real compositional problem. Because the score, the as-of date, and the trust word are welded into one typographic unit, a future cron only needs to mutate three strings per row and the layout stays inert. The v1-static-to-cron upgrade path is genuinely zero-layout-risk.

Rejecting a two-up grid for the list surface is the correct call for the cross-benchmark comparison use case. The Pulse Index two-up works because trends do not need a shared right-edge column; benchmarks need one. A single-column layout with an aligned score spine is the right departure from the Trends precedent, and the proposal names the exact tradeoff.

The four-word trust enum (Active, Near ceiling, Contested, Dated) is the strongest literacy design decision in the proposal. It externalizes editorial judgment into a machine-readable, required field, which means a missing or stale trust state fails loud rather than silently rendering a clean number. That is the failure mode the research explicitly warns about.

The proposal actually read the research data. It correctly identifies that: arena.ai Elo top-3 is a near-statistical tie (confidence intervals overlap); FrontierMath carries an OpenAI-funding controversy; MMLU-Pro's TIGER-Lab board is stale (last update March 2026, not June 2026); HLE has a contested answer-quality finding from FutureHouse; and SWE-bench Verified has a confirmed-but-complicated deprecation story. These are not generic caveats, they come from the verified JSON.
Color-blind safety is handled correctly: trust state is the WORD first and gold second, never gold alone. This is the correct design for a literacy surface aimed at a general student audience.

The detail page's layered-disclosure order (Why you should care BEFORE methodology) is pedagogically sound for a mixed non-expert audience. Burying the payoff below jargon is the standard failure mode for this kind of page, and the proposal explicitly avoids it.
risks: The .trend-detail-split grid in the real codebase (trend-detail-client.tsx line 134) runs at maxWidth 800px, not 1080px. The proposal assumes the detail page uses the full 1080px content frame, but the existing split is narrower and single-purpose. Reusing the CSS class without auditing its max-width means the benchmarks detail page may render noticeably narrower than the list page, breaking the visual continuity the proposal promises.

The proposal invents a two-filter-row header (trust axis tabs + domain tags) above the list. CategoryTab in trends-client.tsx is wired to a single filter dimension (category). A second orthogonal filter axis (domain) would require either a new filter state machine or a combined filter UI that the existing CategoryTab component does not support. The proposal calls this 'reused verbatim' but it is not verbatim at all; it is a new two-axis filter problem.

The struck-through score for near-ceiling benchmarks (HumanEval at 0.55 opacity with strikethrough) is a UI pattern the codebase has never used. The proposal names no existing CSS class for this. It will require new authored styles, and more importantly, a student who does not know what a strikethrough on a percentage means will be confused, not enlightened. The near-ceiling concept needs an inline plain-English explanation, not just a visual treatment.

The right-rail 'pinned in view' claim for the detail page implies position: sticky or similar. The existing trend-detail-split is NOT pinned; both columns scroll together. Making the leader panel truly pinned requires a new layout wrapper with overflow handling that does not exist in the codebase, and on narrow viewports the collapse at <=720px (globals.css line 464) already drops the split entirely. The proposal overpromises 'reused verbatim' on a layout behavior that is not in the source.

The data completeness claim is overstated. The round-2 research JSON explicitly flags FrontierMath with needsRecheck (Epoch AI is mid-review of the benchmark and the verifier returned medium confidence), LiveCodeBench has no verified leader table at all (JS-rendered board + 6.6MB data JSON unparseable), and MMLU-Pro's board is stale as of March 2026, not June 2026. The proposal's 'Leaders as of June 2026, checked by hand' global header is not supportable for at least two of the 13 benchmarks in v1, and the proposal does not surface this discrepancy between the header promise and the actual data state.

The 'near-tie spanning line' for arena.ai Elo is a conditional rendering path that requires a data field to signal when the tie state is active. The proposal says the trust enum handles this, but 'Contested' and 'near-tie' are different states: a board can be Contested (OpenAI funding controversy) without having statistically tied leaders, or have a near-tie without being Contested. The design conflates these two orthogonal conditions into one trust word, which will cause incorrect rendering for FrontierMath (Contested, clean ranking) versus arena.ai (not Contested, near-tie). A separate boolean for near-tie is needed and not specified.

The gold ThinBar recolor on the Watch out for eyebrow is the one structural recolor in an otherwise token-pure system. The proposal acknowledges this but treats it as a small exception. In practice, --color-gold on an editorial surface that uses Quizlet blue (#4255FF) as its accent is a palette collision that the existing editorial pages never face. The gold is used for staleness banners in the dark-theme trend tracker, but in the warm #FAF9F5 light editorial surface, --color-gold (#B8860C in light theme) sits much closer to amber-warm than to a warning state, and may read as decorative rather than cautionary to students who have not seen it used as a warning elsewhere on the site.

The proposal specifies --text-lg (20px) for the score hero in the list rows and the detail leader panel. In the existing TrendCell, --text-xl (24px) is the trend name hero. Using a smaller size for what the proposal calls 'the visual hero of the page' means the score will actually be smaller than benchmark names in the list rows, which directly contradicts the 'number-forward' intent. The score should be at or above the name size to read as the dominant element.
critique.verdict: A strong editorial literacy direction with a well-grounded component vocabulary and a genuinely good pedagogy call (caveats welded to numbers, not buried below them), but it contains four buildability claims that do not survive contact with the actual codebase: the detail split's real max-width is 800px not 1080px, the two-axis filter is not a verbatim reuse, the right-rail sticky behavior does not exist in the source, and the near-tie state needs a separate boolean that the trust enum alone cannot carry. Fix those four before handing this to a developer.

proposal.persona: The Teacher, literacy-first. My non-negotiable: the "why it matters" and the caveat lead; the number is supporting evidence and never the headline. A contested number shown plainly misleads a non-expert worse than no number at all. Every design move lowers the barrier to one true, repeatable sentence a student can say out loud after eight seconds on the list and after three minutes on a detail page. The student is curious and capable but assumes nothing; I never make them hunt for the "as of" date, never let a clean leaderboard imply false confidence, and never define jargon with jargon.
proposal.bigIdea: Invert the leaderboard. Every other AI benchmark surface leads with the number and buries the caveat in a footnote; we lead with what the test is really asking and whether you can trust the answer, and we let the number arrive last, dated and qualified, as evidence rather than verdict. The organizing primitive is STATUS, not score: each benchmark carries one honest state from a fixed four-word vocabulary, ACTIVE, NEAR CEILING, CONTESTED, HISTORICAL, visible on the list row before a click, and that one word color-codes the whole detail page. This is the editorial family's own logic (the Pulse Index sorts by momentum, the digest leads each story with "Why it matters") pointed at contested numeric data. The list reads like a reading list of a knowable field, not a spreadsheet. The detail page is a four-beat lesson (What it really asks, Why you should care, How the score works, What to watch) where the contested-ness has structural parity with the description, never fine print. The leader panel is a dated, interval-bearing, plain-English-glossed top-3 that refuses to crown a false winner when ranks 1 and 2 overlap. The whole surface teaches one literacy skill: a benchmark number is meaningless without its date, its baseline, and its caveat, and the design makes all three travel with the number even in a screenshot. v1 ships static with dated numbers; the weekly leader-line cron only swaps the data inside an already dated panel, so nothing about the design changes when it goes live.
proposal.listSurface:
A single-column hairline index at 1040px (--maxw-content), not the Pulse Index two-up grid. The two-up grid is right when entries are peers you scan and pick from; here the student must read one honest status word per row, and a single column keeps the STATUS column vertically aligned so the eye scans trust-state straight down the page. That is the literacy payoff the two-up would scatter.

Page header reuses the digest/Trends pattern exactly: SectionEyebrow "State of the Models" (11px uppercase, 0.18em, --color-text-3), an H1 "Benchmarks" at clamp(34px,4vw,44px) weight 600, and one plain deck line in --color-text-2 that states the thesis in a sentence: "The tests labs use to claim progress. Most are gamed, saturated, or contested, and the honest picture is more useful than the leaderboard." Below the deck, four ghost-text filter tabs reusing the CategoryTab component verbatim, but the axis is TRUST not topic: All, Active, Contested, Historical. Topic (coding/reasoning/math/multimodal) lives as a quiet metadata atom, not the primary filter, because a non-expert needs "can I trust this" before "what domain is this."

Each row is a CSS grid, "44px minmax(0,1fr) 150px", separated by a single 1px --color-border top rule, 28px vertical padding (the digest row rhythm). Left cell: a soft tile-tinted ordinal badge, 28px circle, --tile-*-bg / --tile-*-fg, tabular-nums, lifted from TrendCell, where the tile is chosen by STATUS not topic (sage=ACTIVE, gold=CONTESTED, stone=HISTORICAL, steel=NEAR CEILING). Middle cell: the benchmark name as hero at --text-xl (24px) weight 600, -0.02em, wrapped in the editorial-link span so only the name sweeps its underline and nudges on row hover; directly under it, the one-line plain-English "what it really asks" deck at 14px --color-text-2, clamped to one line, taken verbatim from the research "what it measures" field (GPQA Diamond: "Science questions PhD experts get right only about 65 percent of the time, even with Google"). Then a quiet 11px uppercase strip, max three atoms, dot-separated like the digest: topic tag, score TYPE (Pass-rate, Elo, Accuracy) so students never compare an Elo to a percentage, nothing else. Right cell (the move): a fixed-width STATUS column holding the one honest word as a tinted SectionEyebrow-style label (CONTESTED in --color-gold/--color-gold-soft, NEAR CEILING in --color-slate, HISTORICAL in stone, ACTIVE in a muted green) plus, on a second 11px line, the single most important caveat in four words ("o3 score self-reported", "numbers from Dec 2024", "ranks 1-2 are tied"). The thesis made scannable: top-to-bottom a student learns, before clicking, that HumanEval is NEAR CEILING, SWE-bench Verified is CONTESTED, FrontierMath is CONTESTED, LiveCodeBench is ACTIVE. Deliberately NO score on the row. Showing "1510" or "87.5%" here is the exact false-confidence failure the brief warns against; the number needs a date, a baseline, and a caveat to mean anything, and the row cannot carry all three honestly, so it carries none. Hover animates only the name (editorial-link sweep + arrow nudge), never the row background; rows are index entries, not buttons.
proposal.detailSurface:
A two-column split at desktop width: left is the four-beat lesson (prose), right is the leader panel, divided by the existing 1px vertical rule primitive (.trend-detail-split / .trend-detail-rule, which already collapses the rule and stacks to one column at <=720px in globals.css). Reusing that class is free and gives correct mobile behavior with the prose first in source order.

Top of the left column, before any number: a BackLink to /benchmarks (the digest component verbatim), then the STATUS word rendered large as the page's tone-setter, a tinted eyebrow plus a one-sentence plain gloss of what that status MEANS, because "saturated" as a bare word is jargon. For NEAR CEILING: "Top models now score so close together that the gaps are inside the measurement noise, so this test no longer tells you which model is better." Then the H1 benchmark name, then a single bold lead sentence (Wikipedia/OWID lead-section discipline, bus-stop test: a student repeats it accurately after one read).

The four beats are four named sections, each opened by a SectionEyebrow, separated by HairRule (both digest primitives), in this fixed order so layered disclosure pays off: (1) WHAT IT REALLY ASKS, the plain definition plus one verbatim or paraphrased EXAMPLE TASK in a slightly inset block so the student feels the difficulty before seeing any accuracy figure (a real Google-proof GPQA question; "fix this real bug a developer actually filed" for SWE-bench). (2) WHY YOU SHOULD CARE, the research "why a normal person should care" line, placed second so a student who bails early still leaves with the payoff, never last. (3) HOW THE SCORE WORKS, metric, scale, who runs it, and crucially the baselines that make a number legible (chance is 25 percent, PhD experts hit about 81 percent on Diamond, prior SOTA was X) so no figure ever appears without its anchor. (4) WHAT TO WATCH, given full structural parity with beat 3, never a footnote: contamination, saturation, the named controversy as a real linked resource the way Epoch links its FrontierMath funding post, not a parenthetical. This beat's eyebrow and HairRule render in --color-gold for any CONTESTED benchmark, so the caveat section is visually load-bearing. Catalog cross-links are embedded inline at the exact sentence where a concept first appears (the editorial-link from the digest's "Related in the catalog" block: when the prose says "graduate-level reasoning," the word links to the Reasoning concept), plus a closing "To understand this, read" cluster, so the page is a reading path into the catalog, which is TCO's actual literacy goal.
proposal.leaderPanel:
A hairline-separated dated top-3, NOT a sortable table, living in the right column (or full-width below the lesson on mobile). It is stuck to the lesson by a per-panel dated stamp, never a global page footer date: a SectionEyebrow reading "LEADERS AS OF JUN 10 2026" sitting on a HairRule directly above the three rows, so a student who screenshots only the panel still captures the date. Each row is the existing 2-column "rank badge | content" split: left, the soft tile-tinted ordinal (1, 2, 3) reusing the TrendCell badge; right, the model name as hero (Hanken 16px weight 600, "claude-fable-5"), the lab in muted 12px ("Anthropic"), and only then the score on its own line with three things welded into one visual unit so they can never be separated: the figure, its interval, and its plain gloss. For arena: "1510 plus or minus 11  ·  top of 360+ models". The interval is small and muted (the Arena.ai "1510 +/- 11" gold standard) so the headline reads first but uncertainty is never hidden, and "plus or minus" is spelled out, never a glyph, for non-experts and the no-dash rule.

Three honesty mechanisms are built into the panel structure, not bolted on: (a) when two intervals overlap, a TIE BRACKET, a 1px hairline connecting ranks 1 and 2 with an 11px "statistical tie" label, drawn straight from the ThinBar/gauge geometry, so the panel literally refuses to render a false winner (arena ranks 1-2 overlap as of Jun 2026 and the design must show that, not hide it). (b) A self-reported or disputed figure carries an inline tinted "self-reported" / "disputed" chip in the reading flow next to the score, never a hover tooltip, because tooltips are invisible on mobile and to skimmers (FrontierMath o3's 25.2 percent is the canonical case). (c) Every score carries its inline baseline gloss so magnitude is never naked. When v1 has no trustworthy current top-3 for a benchmark (true for most, per the research, only arena has live verified leaders), the panel does NOT fabricate a clean three; it shows an honest "No reliable current leaderboard. The most cited figure is a HISTORICAL anchor:" state with the dated o3 / FrontierMath baseline. That honest-empty state is a first-class design element, the single most teacherly thing on the surface, because inventing a leaderboard to fill a slot is the exact lie the page exists to inoculate against. When the weekly BenchmarkLeaderCache cron lands, it swaps the data inside this already-dated, already-interval-bearing panel; zero structural change, the additive path the spec wants.

referencePick.name: Our World in Data indicator pages
referencePick.why: It is the only mainstream reference that solves my exact problem: contested numeric data for a curious non-expert, where the number is never allowed to stand alone. OWID welds source and year inline to every figure (Source: World Bank (2023) beside the number, not in a footer), gives every indicator a named Data Quality and Definitions section with structural parity to the description rather than a footnote, opens with a jargon-free lead sentence, and cross-links related indicators at the point of first use. That maps one-to-one onto my four moves: the per-panel dated stamp (their inline source-plus-year), the WHAT TO WATCH beat at full weight (their Data Quality section), the bus-stop lead sentence, and inline catalog cross-links. Crucially OWID proves to a skeptical student audience that leading with the caveat reads as more credible, not less, which is the entire bet of The Honest Scoreboard. I pick it over Arena.ai (whose 1510 plus or minus 11 inline interval I borrow for the leader rows but whose practitioner-built, undated, caveat-light board is a negative reference) and over Typewolf (whose row rhythm the Pulse Index already adapted) because OWID gets the literacy stance right, not just the layout.

proposal.repoFit: Reuses, near-zero new primitives. From globals.css: --maxw-content (1040) and --pad-page-* for the frame; the full type ramp (--text-xl for hero names, --text-xs for metadata, raw 11px SectionEyebrow micro-labels as the editorial surfaces already do); --color-text / -2 / -3 for size-and-opacity-only hierarchy; --color-border / --color-border-subtle for every hairline; the tile-* pastel pairs for ordinal badges and STATUS chips (sage=ACTIVE, gold-soft=CONTESTED, stone=HISTORICAL, steel=NEAR CEILING); --color-gold / --color-gold-soft for the CONTESTED eyebrow, the WHAT TO WATCH accent, and the existing 36h stale-banner pattern reused for a benchmark-level staleness note; --radius-2 / 999 for badges and chips. The page wraps in data-surface="editorial" (Quizlet #4255FF, Hanken) inside the (main) data-theme="light" shell, exactly like TrendsClient and DigestClient. From trends-client.tsx: SectionEyebrow, ArrowRight, CategoryTab (re-pointed from topic to TRUST axis), the tile-tinted tabular-nums ordinal badge, the editorial-link sweep block (copied by value, as that file already does), usePrefersReducedMotion, and the ThinBar/MomentumGauge geometry repurposed as the leader-panel TIE BRACKET. From digest-client.tsx: BackLink, HairRule, the numbered-row grid rhythm, the "Why it matters" eyebrow-over-prose block (becomes WHY YOU SHOULD CARE), the "Related in the catalog" inline editorial-link cluster (becomes the catalog cross-links), and the gold-soft Banner for staleness. From .trend-detail-split / .trend-detail-rule in globals.css: the detail-page two-column split with the 1px vertical rule and its <=720px single-column collapse, free and correct on mobile. Data fits the spec model exactly: a static curated Benchmark body (four beats, example task, baselines, status, catalog slugs) authored from docs/research/benchmarks-research*.json, plus a BenchmarkLeaderCache leader line; v1 hardcodes dated leaders, the weekly cron later writes the cache the panel already reads. New CSS is one rule, the TIE BRACKET, and it is geometry only.
proposal.tradeoffs: Honest costs. (1) No score on the list row is the boldest call and some users will feel the index is "missing" the thing a leaderboard is for; I accept that friction because a row cannot carry a number with its date, baseline, and caveat at once, and a naked number is the precise harm the brief names. Mitigation: the STATUS word plus four-word caveat gives the row real information, just not false precision. (2) Sorting by TRUST not topic is opinionated; a student hunting "the coding benchmarks" must use the quiet topic metadata atom or scan, not a primary filter. I judge "can I trust this" to outrank "what domain" for a literacy-first audience, but a topic sub-filter is the obvious additive concession if usage disagrees. (3) The single column scrolls more than the Pulse two-up for 8-14 entries; I trade compactness for a vertically aligned STATUS column, the scannability that makes the thesis legible. (4) The honest-empty leader state risks reading as unfinished rather than principled; it needs careful copy and the dated HISTORICAL anchor beside it to read as deliberate. (5) Four named beats plus a leader panel is heavier to author per benchmark than a table row, and demands real editorial work on baselines and example tasks; that is load-bearing, not decoration, but it is why this is effort-M not effort-S. (6) The STATUS vocabulary is a forced four-way classification and some benchmarks are genuinely two states at once (HumanEval is NEAR CEILING and somewhat HISTORICAL); the rule is pick the one a student most needs to hear and let WHAT TO WATCH hold the nuance. (7) The TIE BRACKET only earns its complexity for benchmarks with published intervals (arena); for pass-rate benchmarks without intervals it does not render, which is correct but means the panel has two visual modes to maintain.

strengths: Repo fit is genuinely strong on the token layer. Every CSS variable the proposal calls out (tile-*, --color-gold-soft, --color-border, --color-accent, --text-xl, --text-xs, --maxw-content) exists exactly as named in globals.css. The trend-detail-split / trend-detail-rule breakpoint collapse at 720px is real, free, and already correct for mobile. SectionEyebrow, HairRule, BackLink, CategoryTab, the editorial-link sweep, and the ordinal badge are all in working code. Reuse claims are not aspirational; they are accurate.

The STATUS vocabulary (ACTIVE, NEAR CEILING, CONTESTED, HISTORICAL) is the single strongest design decision. It translates a four-word trust signal before any click, which is exactly what a non-expert needs and what every mainstream AI benchmark surface omits. The research data actually supports this categorization: SWE-bench Verified genuinely is CONTESTED (OpenAI dropped it, tests flawed, board frozen at Feb 2026), GPQA Diamond genuinely is NEAR CEILING (top models clustered within 1-2 percentage points, inside inter-run variance), LMArena genuinely is ACTIVE. The vocabulary maps cleanly onto real distinctions in the verified data.

The leader panel's honesty mechanics are load-bearing and well-designed. The per-panel 'LEADERS AS OF JUN 10 2026' dated stamp directly above the rows, the tie bracket for overlapping confidence intervals (the arena.ai top-two case: 1510 plus or minus 11 vs 1504 plus or minus 4 is a near-statistical tie confirmed in the research), and the honest-empty state for benchmarks without trustworthy live leaders (which the round-2 JSON confirms is most of them) are all substantive design moves, not decorations. The FrontierMath self-reported chip for o3's 25.2 percent is grounded in an actual verified fact (Epoch AI funding disclosure, OpenAI privileged access). These mechanics match real data problems.

The four-beat lesson structure (WHAT IT REALLY ASKS, WHY YOU SHOULD CARE, HOW THE SCORE WORKS, WHAT TO WATCH) places WHY YOU SHOULD CARE second, which is correct for the audience. A student who bails at beat 2 leaves with the payoff. Putting WHAT TO WATCH at beat 4 with structural parity (gold rule for CONTESTED benchmarks) is a genuine improvement over footnote caveats. The OWID reference is apt: it is the only mainstream source that treats contested numeric data for non-experts the way the proposal treats benchmark scores.

The single-column list over the Pulse Index two-up grid is the right call for this data shape. The two-up grid works when entries are peers to scan and pick from; here the STATUS column needs to read vertically down the page. Switching from topic-first to trust-first filtering is defensible for a literacy audience. The decision to omit the score from the list row is correct given that a naked number without date, baseline, and caveat is the precise harm the brief names, and the row cannot carry all three.
risks: The tile color mapping for STATUS is broken against the actual token set. The proposal assigns sage=ACTIVE, gold=CONTESTED, stone=HISTORICAL, steel=NEAR CEILING. But in the light theme, --color-gold is #B8860C (a dark amber text color) and --color-gold-soft is #FBEFCE (the background tint). The proposal conflates the text color token with the background token throughout the leader panel and STATUS chip descriptions, saying 'CONTESTED in --color-gold/--color-gold-soft' as if they are interchangeable. In practice every chip needs both a background (the -soft or -bg variant) and a foreground (the base color), and the proposal never specifies the pairing. The tile- tokens (tile-gold-bg, tile-gold-fg, tile-sage-bg, tile-sage-fg) are the correct chip palette but the proposal mixes tile-* tokens with color-* tokens as if they are the same thing. A developer reading this spec would need to resolve the ambiguity before any chip renders correctly.

The honest-empty leader state, the proposal's 'single most teacherly thing on the surface,' is at serious risk of reading as a broken or unfinished page rather than a deliberate editorial stance. The round-2 research confirms that only the arena.ai text board has a verified current top-3. All other benchmarks show historical anchors (December 2024 for ARC-AGI-1, November 2024 for FrontierMath, March 2026 for MMLU-Pro which is already 3 months stale relative to frontiers). The proposal offers one line of copy ('No reliable current leaderboard. The most cited figure is a HISTORICAL anchor:') but the bulk of the v1 set will land in this state. That is 10 to 12 of 13 benchmarks showing a variant of the honest-empty panel. The cumulative effect may undermine the site's credibility with a student who expected live data, regardless of how principled the decision is. The proposal acknowledges this as tradeoff (4) but does not offer a concrete copy solution beyond the one-liner, and that is insufficient for the volume of honest-empty states v1 will actually display.

The four-beat lesson authoring burden is underestimated. The proposal calls this 'effort-M not effort-S' and notes it 'demands real editorial work on baselines and example tasks.' But the research data reveals how deep that work goes. GPQA Diamond requires a verbatim Google-proof example question (the research confirms these exist but they are licensed from the paper). SWE-bench Verified needs a concrete real GitHub issue used as the example task. FrontierMath's WHAT IT REALLY ASKS beat must explain research-grade number theory to a non-expert in a brief inset block. For 13 benchmarks, each with an example task, a plain-English scoring explanation, accurate baselines (the research flags that the 65 percent PhD expert figure must not be silently mapped to Diamond where experts score 81 percent), and a WHAT TO WATCH beat with linked primary sources, this is closer to effort-L editorial work before a single line of code ships.

The TIE BRACKET is described as geometry reused from ThinBar and MomentumGauge but neither ThinBar nor the gauge geometry actually implements anything structurally similar to a 1px hairline connecting two rows with an 11px label. MomentumGauge (confirmed in the code) is a dot-on-track SVG-free span arrangement. The TIE BRACKET would require new CSS absolute positioning inside the leader panel, a z-index context, and careful handling of the two-row case vs the three-row case. The claim that 'new CSS is one rule' is not credible; the bracket is a new component with at minimum 8 to 12 CSS properties for correct rendering across viewport widths, plus a conditional render path. This is the proposal's least honest engineering claim.

The trust-first filter axis (All, Active, Contested, Historical) versus topic-first is the right call for the audience but creates a UX gap that the proposal does not fully resolve. A TCO student who arrives at /benchmarks having heard about 'the coding benchmark everyone uses' or 'the math benchmark that o3 aced' has no path to find it except scanning or reading metadata atoms. The proposal's mitigation ('a topic sub-filter is the obvious additive concession') treats it as a phase-2 concern, but the navigation pattern for a non-expert who knows the domain but not the trust state is left unsolved in v1. Given that 'SWE-bench' and 'GPQA' are the names students will arrive with from news coverage, a search or secondary sort should be in v1 scope, not deferred.

The proposal conflates 'no color-coded row background on hover' (correct for editorial rows) with 'editorial-link sweep only on the name' and then places a full-cell invisible link (position: absolute, inset: 0) as the click target, exactly as TrendCell does. But the editorial-link sweep is a CSS pseudo-element on the name span, and the full-cell absolute link sits on top of it. In TrendCell this works because the whole cell is clickable. Here the proposal says 'hover animates only the name' but if the full-cell link is used (the obvious implementation), hover state is on the cell, not the name span, and triggering the sweep from a cell-level hover requires a CSS sibling or descendant combinator, which the proposal does not specify. This is a concrete implementation gap that has bitten the trends surface before (the codebase survey notes this pattern).
critique.verdict: The literacy stance and STATUS vocabulary are the right design bets, grounded in verified data, and the token reuse is accurate, but the proposal understates the editorial authoring volume (honest-empty states will dominate v1), misclaims the TIE BRACKET as a one-rule addition, and leaves the tile/color token pairing ambiguous enough to cause implementation errors before the first row renders.

proposal.persona: The Magazine Index designer. I treat /benchmarks as a curated editorial field guide, not a leaderboard product. Benchmarks are a bounded, knowable set of about a dozen contested measuring sticks, and the honest story (most are gamed, saturated, or disputed) is more interesting than any ranking. My job is to give that field strong reading rhythm, a Typewolf-style contents-page cadence, and a visible trust grammar so a non-technical TCO student can scan it in eight seconds and never mistake a dated or self-reported number for settled fact.
proposal.designLanguageName: The Standings — a State-of-the-Models field guide. "Standings" because it borrows the visual literacy of sports standings (rank badge, dated snapshot, categorical state labels, score-plus-record gloss) while stripping every practitioner affordance (sortable columns, raw Elo math, confidence-interval notation). It is a sibling of The Pulse Index, not a clone: where Pulse reads as momentum, Standings reads as a ranked, contested field worth browsing.
proposal.bigIdea: One orthogonal axis the Pulse Index does not have: TRUST STATUS, made a first-class visual citizen of every row. The Pulse Index sorts by category and reads momentum; The Standings reads a field where the headline question is not "who is winning" but "can you believe this number." Every benchmark carries a coarse state label (LIVE, NEAR CEILING, CONTESTED, RETIRED) rendered as a soft tinted chip in the metadata strip, visible at list level before any click, so a student learns that 90% on a saturated test is meaningless while 47% on Humanity's Last Exam is frontier. The number is never shown naked: it always travels with its as-of date, a one-line human-baseline gloss, and (when the leaders[] flags say so) an inline SELF-REPORTED or DISPUTED mark in the reading flow, never behind a hover. v1 is fully static (the data already lives in benchmarks-research-round2.json: leaders, asOfDate, selfReported, disputed, status); a later weekly cron only swaps the leader lines and re-stamps the date, changing data not structure.
proposal.listSurface: A Typewolf two-up contents page reusing the exact .trend-grid (repeat(2,1fr), columnGap 48, single 1px top rule, collapses to one column at 720px). Header is the established editorial stack: SectionEyebrow "State of the Models", an H1 "Benchmarks" at clamp(34-44px) weight 600, and a one-line deck. Below it, a row of ghost-text filter tabs (the CategoryTab primitive, 2px accent sweep) but filtering on the new axis: All / Reasoning / Coding / Math / Multimodal / Human preference, with live counts. Each cell (28px vertical padding, 1px bottom rule, full-cell Link, trendCellIn entrance with capped 18ms stagger) reads top to bottom: (1) a soft tile-tinted ordinal badge (28px circle, --tile-indigo-bg/-fg, tabular figures) plus an UPPERCASE 11px category kicker in that tile's fg color, exactly the TrendCell head; (2) the benchmark NAME as the hero at --text-xl weight 600 with the editorial-link underline-sweep on cell hover; (3) the deck line, which IS the whatItMeasures sentence, clamped to two lines at 14px in --color-text-2; (4) the metadata strip at 11px uppercase letter-spaced, capped at three atoms separated by a centered dot, never a dash: the TRUST chip first (LIVE in sage, NEAR CEILING in slate, CONTESTED in amber/--color-gold-soft, RETIRED in stone), then a quiet leader line "LEADS: GEMINI 3.1 PRO" with no score, then "AS OF MAR 2026". No raw percentage on the list at all: that is deliberate, because a bare number with no baseline misleads, and forcing the click is the literacy gate. The whole index of about thirteen benchmarks fits in roughly one and a half laptop viewports, communicating "this is a complete, learnable set, not a feed."
proposal.detailSurface: A single flowing editorial brief inside the trend-detail-split (minmax(0,1.35fr) / 1px rule / minmax(0,1fr), columnGap 48, collapses at 720px), reusing the trend-detail headline entrance and HairRule dividers. Top: arrow-left breadcrumb to /benchmarks, a category kicker plus the TRUST chip inline, then the name at clamp(40-48px) and a deck that is the whyCare sentence promoted to the top (the "so what" before any mechanics, per the research pitfall about answering what without so-what). LEFT column is the article in fixed parallel slots, each a 16px sentence-case ArticleSection subhead over 15px/1.7 prose, in the research's exact order: "What it measures", "Why it matters", "How scoring works" (renders the scoring.metric in plain English plus a glossed term linking inline to the catalog via editorial-link), then an "Example task" slot rendered as a left-rule-indented block so difficulty is felt not just told, then "What to watch" given STRUCTURAL PARITY (its own subhead, not a footnote) and marked by a 2px ThinBar in --color-gold instead of accent so saturation/contamination caveats read as a caveat before the words are processed. RIGHT rail is the live LEADER panel (below). Catalog cross-links live both inline at first mention and as a closing accent-soft ConceptChip cluster reusing the trend ConceptChip, mapping relatedConcepts[] to /concepts slugs, so the page ends by handing the student the concept that explains the test. Mixed-format dates are normalized to "Mon YYYY" for display; the verbatim asOfDate stays in a title attribute for provenance.
proposal.leaderPanel: A hairline-separated top-3 in the right rail, framed by a per-panel dated stamp on its own rule: a SectionEyebrow reading "LEADERS AS OF MAR 2026" (the freshness travels with the data, not a page footer, so a screenshot carries its own date). Three rows, each a 2-column split echoing the digest row: a small soft rank badge (1/2/3, --tile-stone for 2 and 3, --tile-indigo for 1) left of the content. Content per row: model name as a 15px weight-600 hero, lab in 12px --color-text-2 below, then the score as the free-text string from leaders[].score (it often already carries its own "+/- 1.96" interval, which I keep verbatim as the gold-standard inline-uncertainty display), and under it a 12px gloss line anchoring the number to a reference point ("PhD experts: ~81%" / "random chance: 25%" / "prior frontier: 88%") so the magnitude is interpretable. Trust flags render INLINE in the reading flow, never on hover: leaders[].selfReported true paints a small SELF-REPORTED chip in --color-slate-soft, leaders[].disputed true paints a DISPUTED chip in --color-gold-soft, and statistical near-ties get a quiet "near tie" mark so ranks within one interval do not read as a clean ordering. Each row's score links to leaders[].sourceUrl via an arrow-square-out source chip. When status is RETIRED or NEAR CEILING the panel shows a one-line "why the gap at the top is noise" caption above the three rows, so a student never reads a saturated 96.3% as a meaningful win. The structure is data-agnostic: the weekly cron rewrites the three lines and the stamp, nothing else.

referencePick.name: Typewolf — 50 Best Sites / Favorite Sites index
referencePick.why: It is the canonical two-up contents page my list surface already inherits in code (.trend-grid maps to it one to one): soft tinted ordinal badge, the name as the typographic hero standing in for Typewolf's screenshot, an 11px uppercase metadata strip in the slot Typewolf uses for FONTS, and a single 1px rule with air instead of cards. I borrow its restraint (no chrome, hover only the name) and repurpose the metadata strip to carry the TRUST chip plus the dated leader line, which is exactly the contested-data signal the spec demands, surfaced at the right moment (before the click) rather than buried in a disclaimer. I deliberately do NOT pick a live leaderboard like Arena.ai or Artificial Analysis as the primary reference: those are practitioner products that bury the as-of date and treat all numbers as equally trustworthy, the precise failure this surface exists to correct.

proposal.repoFit: Almost zero new primitives. Reuses verbatim: the .trend-grid and .trend-detail-split CSS hooks with their 720px collapse (globals.css), the data-surface="editorial" wrapper for Quizlet blue #4255FF + Hanken, SectionEyebrow (11px uppercase), the editorial-link underline-sweep + arrow nudge, HairRule, ThinBar (gold fill for the watch-out marker, same component dashboard uses), the CategoryTab ghost tab with 2px accent sweep, the soft tile ordinal badge + category kicker from TrendCell, ConceptChip (accent-soft, arrow-right) and the breadcrumb/StaleBanner from trend-detail-client, the arrow-square-out source chip and dated-eyebrow StoryRow pattern from the Recent signals log, and the trendCellIn / trendHeadlineIn entrances gated by usePrefersReducedMotion. Tokens: --tile-* pastels for category badges and the trust chips (sage=LIVE, slate=NEAR CEILING, gold-soft=CONTESTED, stone=RETIRED), --color-gold for the watch-out ThinBar, --color-text/-2/-3 opacity tiers for hierarchy. Data: benchmarks-research-round2.json already provides whatItMeasures, whyMatters, scoring.metric, watchOutFor, leaders[] with score/asOfDate/selfReported/disputed, whyCare, relatedConcepts, and a coarse status. New work is only: a tiny status->chip map, a date-normalizer to "Mon YYYY", a per-benchmark human-baseline gloss string (hand-authored, one line each), and two route files under app/(main). Net: it ships static today and is cron-ready by construction.
proposal.tradeoffs: Honest costs. (1) The list shows NO score, only LEADS + AS OF + trust chip. That is opinionated and some users will want the number up front; I trade instant gratification for the literacy gate, because a bare percentage without a baseline is the single most misleading thing this page could do, and the click cost is one tap. (2) The two-up grid is dense; with about thirteen benchmarks plus a three-atom metadata strip it risks feeling busy if any deck line wraps to three lines, so the deck must hard-clamp to two and the strip must hard-cap at three atoms (anything more, like score-type, goes to the detail page). (3) The human-baseline gloss is hand-authored per benchmark, not in the JSON, so it is the one slot that can go stale or be subjective and needs an editor's eye; I judge that worth it because it is the highest-leverage line for a non-expert. (4) Coarse trust chips (four states) flatten real nuance: a benchmark can be both NEAR CEILING and partly CONTESTED, and I show only the dominant state on the list, pushing the full picture to the detail "What to watch" slot. (5) Reusing Pulse's grid risks the two surfaces blurring together; the distinct trust-chip color grammar, the score-plus-baseline leader panel, and the gold caveat ThinBar are what earn Standings its own identity. (6) Free-text score strings and mixed-format dates from the JSON mean display normalization is best-effort; I keep the verbatim string (intervals included) and normalize only the date label, with the raw value preserved in a title attribute.
