strengths: Repo fit is exceptionally well-scoped. The proposal correctly identifies that .trend-grid, .trend-detail-split, CategoryTab, SectionEyebrow, ThinBar, HairRule, ConceptChip, StaleBanner, StoryRow, arrow-square-out, trendCellIn, and usePrefersReducedMotion all exist verbatim in the codebase and can be composed without new primitive work. The token inventory (--tile-sage-bg/fg for LIVE, --color-gold-soft for CONTESTED, --tile-stone for RETIRED) maps exactly to globals.css light-theme values.

The trust-status axis is the single most valuable design decision in this proposal, and it is the right answer for an AI-literacy audience. Making CONTESTED, NEAR CEILING, and RETIRED first-class visual citizens at list level, before any click, is precisely what a non-expert needs to avoid treating a saturated 96.3% HumanEval score as meaningful. The research data strongly supports this: the JSON carries status, selfReported, disputed, and needsRecheck fields, plus the FrontierMath needsRecheck flag and the HLE answer-quality caveat, all of which the chip grammar can surface directly.

Hiding the raw score from the list is the right literacy call and is honestly argued in the tradeoffs section. The proposal correctly diagnoses that a bare percentage without a human baseline is the single most misleading thing the page can show, and it is consistent with the editorial brand's preference for explanation over raw data.

The leader panel's design handles the actual data shape correctly. The JSON's leaders[] already carries score as a free-text string (e.g., '46.44% (+/-1.96)'), asOfDate, selfReported, and disputed per-row. The proposal keeps the verbatim score string (preserving the confidence interval inline) and normalizes only the date label, which is accurate and avoids inventing a false precision. The dated eyebrow 'LEADERS AS OF MON YYYY' traveling with the panel, not in a footer, is a genuine improvement over how most leaderboard UIs handle staleness.

The proposal is honest about data gaps. FrontierMath needsRecheck, HLE answer-error rate, SWE-bench Verified partial deprecation, and ARC-AGI-2 all-self-reported leaders are accurately named as requiring caveat treatment. The design has a structural slot (the gold ThinBar + 'What to watch' section) to surface these without burying them.

The four-state status vocabulary (LIVE, NEAR CEILING, CONTESTED, RETIRED) is learnable in one pass for a non-expert and maps plausibly to real benchmark states in the dataset. HumanEval and MMLU-Pro map to NEAR CEILING, SWE-bench Verified to CONTESTED, ARC-AGI-2 (public leaderboard) to CONTESTED, and HLE to LIVE despite the answer-quality caveat.
risks: The color-token mapping for trust chips is underspecified and will collide in practice. The proposal assigns --tile-sage for LIVE and --tile-slate for NEAR CEILING, but --tile-slate does not exist in globals.css. The real token is --tile-steel (steel-bg: #D8DEEC, steel-fg: #38486D). This is not a trivial gap: the developer will either invent a token (breaking the token system) or silently fall back to a wrong color. The proposal should have read globals.css before specifying tile names.

The two-column grid at 13 benchmarks produces an odd-cell tail. The .trend-grid is repeat(2,1fr) with no explicit handling for an odd last item. In the Trends client, an odd item spans only the left column, leaving a blank right cell. With 13 benchmarks the last row has one lonely cell. The proposal does not address this. The Trends list avoids this because its count is admin-controlled; benchmarks v1 is hardcoded at approximately 13. A CSS grid-column: span 2 on the last odd child, or a deliberate 14th entry, needs to be planned.

The 'no score on the list' rule will confuse a meaningful share of the audience in ways the proposal underestimates. Non-expert students at TCO are unlikely to be misled by a bare percentage in the way a practitioner would be; they have less prior expectation that a 96% means something specific. For them, the more likely failure mode is clicking through expecting an explanation and getting a detail page that still requires them to understand what 'PhD baseline ~81%' means contextually. The human-baseline gloss is the correct remedy, but the proposal concedes it is hand-authored and unstated in the JSON, making it the single most fragile slot in the design.

The detail page's right-rail leader panel is overloaded for a first-time reader. A non-expert student who has never seen a confidence interval will not know what '46.44% (+/-1.96)' means, and the proposal keeps it verbatim. The proposal does provide a gloss line (e.g., 'random chance: 25%'), which partially anchors the number, but the interval itself is unglossed. A GPQA Diamond leader panel showing three models clustered within 1 percentage point, all marked 'near tie', with two SELF-REPORTED chips and one DISPUTED chip, risks reading as a wall of caveats that teaches 'nothing is trustworthy' rather than 'here is how to read contested data'. The proposal notes this risk (tradeoff 4) but has no mitigation beyond the detail 'What to watch' slot.

The four-state chip flattens benchmarks that legitimately carry multiple states simultaneously. FrontierMath is CONTESTED (needsRecheck for pre-correction scores) AND approaching saturation at Tiers 1-3. SWE-bench Verified is CONTESTED (OpenAI dropped it, all top entries are self-reported) but technically still LIVE (the board is active). HLE is LIVE but has a structural answer-quality problem. Assigning a single dominant state forces editorial judgment calls that will be opaque to students and will require re-justification every time a benchmark state shifts. The proposal acknowledges this in tradeoff 4 but offers no governance model for who decides the dominant state and when it changes.

The list surface claims it fits in 'roughly one and a half laptop viewports' but this depends on two-line deck clamp holding reliably. The actual whatItMeasures strings in the JSON are long (HumanEval's is 57 words, TAU-bench's is 45 words). A two-line clamp at 14px in a roughly 440px column (half of 1040px minus 48px gap) clips aggressively and can produce misleading fragment endings mid-sentence. The proposal mandates a hard two-line clamp but does not audit actual string lengths against column width. This is a build risk, not a fatal flaw, but it will require copy editing on several entries.

The cron-ready claim for v1 static data is overstated for some benchmarks. GPQA Diamond has no official leaderboard; its data comes from Artificial Analysis, which provides no stable per-row deep links and no last-updated timestamp. FrontierMath numbers are pre-correction pending Epoch AI's review. ARC-AGI-2 has zero ARC-Prize-verified results, only self-reported aggregator data. A weekly cron refreshing these leader lines has no canonical source to pull from for three of the approximately 13 benchmarks, which means v2 cron work is harder than 'swap leader lines and re-stamp the date' for a meaningful subset of the dataset.
critique.verdict: A strong editorial frame with one token error, one grid edge case, and an audience-complexity gap that the human-baseline gloss partially closes but does not fully solve: build it, fix the --tile-slate phantom before touching CSS, audit the deck strings, and add a one-sentence interval explainer to the leader panel so a first-time reader knows what +/-1.96 means.

synthesis.designLanguageName: The Standings, a State-of-the-Models field guide
synthesis.rationale:
I am picking the Teacher's "Honest Scoreboard" as the spine and grafting the most buildable, best-grounded parts of the other three onto it. The decision rests on the one fact that defines this dataset: per the verified round-2 JSON, almost every benchmark carries a provenance defect (selfReported, disputed, needsRecheck, a null or stale boardLastUpdated, or a split-name caveat baked into the free-text score string). So the organizing axis cannot be "who is winning." It has to be "can you trust this number," and that must be legible before a click. The Teacher's STATUS-first vocabulary is the cleanest expression of that, and three of the four research lenses independently arrived at the same "trust tier visible in the list view" pattern.

From the Teacher I take: the four-word STATUS vocabulary as a required machine-readable field (LIVE, NEAR CEILING, CONTESTED, DATED, matching the data's own status concept and reading in plain English); the four-beat detail lesson with WHY YOU SHOULD CARE placed second so a bailing reader still leaves with the payoff; the per-panel dated stamp; the honest-empty leader state; and WHAT TO WATCH at full structural parity.

From the Data-Forward designer I graft the ScoreStamp atom (score, as-of, and trust flag welded into one inseparable typographic unit so a screenshot always carries its date) plus the required-enum-fails-loud discipline. I reject his core move of a big naked score in the list row (the dataset cannot support a trustworthy bare per-row number) and his "pinned sticky rail" (it does not exist in the codebase).

From the Magazine-Index designer I graft the curated-field-guide framing (a bounded, knowable set, not a feed), the soft-numbered-badge plus hero-name plus three-atom metadata strip, and the rule to keep the verbatim score string but normalize only the date label. I reject his two-up grid for the list, because two columns invite the eye to read a 91 percent next to a 25 percent as a league table when the scores are not comparable.

From the Editorial Minimalist I graft the single-column list (it kills false cross-benchmark comparison), the inline catalog cross-link at first mention of a concept, and the discipline of never letting color carry meaning alone (always pair gold with a literal word).

Net: STATUS is the hero of the list, the welded ScoreStamp is the hero of the leader panel, the four-beat lesson is the body, and the whole thing reuses existing primitives. v1 ships fully static with hardcoded dated leaders. The future weekly cron mutates only the three strings inside each already-dated ScoreStamp, never the layout.

referencePicks.name: Our World in Data, indicator page pattern
referencePicks.why: The gold standard for contested numeric data aimed at non-experts: a plain-sentence definition at the very top, inline Source plus Year fused to every number so the date cannot be screenshotted away, a named Data Quality and Definitions section given real structural weight rather than a footnote, and cross-links to related indicators placed at the point of first use. This maps one to one onto our four-beat detail page, the welded ScoreStamp, the gold WHAT TO WATCH parity section, and the inline catalog handoff, all achieved with typographic hierarchy and restraint, not chrome.

referencePicks.name: Humanity's Last Exam leaderboard, Rank (Upper Bound) metric
referencePicks.why: It solves our exact near-tie problem honestly: instead of faking a clean number one when scores are statistically tied, it reports how many models are significantly better, which collapses to a plain-English line we can write as statistically tied with ranks 2 and 3. We borrow the upper-bound idea but translate it out of statistics into one sentence a non-technical student reads instantly. Paired with Arena.ai's inline 1510 plus or minus 11 only as the micro-pattern for the one Elo board that needs an interval.

referencePicks.name: Typewolf, Favorite Sites index (negative-reference balanced)
referencePicks.why: The canonical numbered-badge plus hero-name plus 11px uppercase metadata-strip row at hairline separators, which the existing Pulse Index already adapted. We borrow the row rhythm and the restraint (hover only the name, no card chrome) but deliberately use one column not two, because Typewolf's entries are comparable peers and our scores are not.

listSurface.concept: A single-column hairline index at --maxw-content (1040px), NOT the Pulse Index two-up grid. The break from two columns is a data-integrity call, not a style choice: reading across rows would invite false comparison of non-comparable scores (91 percent MMLU-Pro next to 25 percent FrontierMath looks like a league table). One column forces vertical reading and aligns the STATUS column straight down the page, which is the literacy payoff. Header is the established editorial stack: SectionEyebrow STATE OF THE MODELS, an H1 Benchmarks at clamp(34px, 4vw, 44px) weight 600, and one plain deck line in --color-text-2 stating the thesis. Below the deck, ONE row of ghost-text CategoryTab filters on the TRUST axis with live counts (All, Live, Contested, Dated; NEAR CEILING folds under a single visible state set if counts are thin). Domain (Reasoning, Coding, Math, Multimodal, Human preference) is a quiet metadata atom inside each row, not a second filter row, to avoid the two-axis empty-intersection problem the critiques flagged. A small free-text search box (reused input styling) is in v1 scope so a student who arrives knowing the name (SWE-bench, GPQA) can find it without scanning. Each row is a CSS grid 44px / minmax(0,1fr) / 150px, separated by one 1px --color-border top rule, 28px vertical padding. LEFT cell: a soft tile-tinted ordinal badge (28px circle, --tile-indigo-bg / --tile-indigo-fg, tabular-nums, position only, never implying rank quality). MIDDLE cell: the benchmark name as hero at --text-xl (24px) weight 600 wrapped in the editorial-link span (only the name sweeps on hover); under it the verbatim whatItMeasures sentence at 14px --color-text-2 clamped to one line; then an 11px uppercase strip capped at TWO atoms dot-separated, the domain tag and the score TYPE (Accuracy, Elo, Pass rate) so students never compare an Elo to a percentage. RIGHT cell, fixed 150px: the STATUS word as a tinted chip (LIVE in tile-sage, NEAR CEILING in tile-steel, CONTESTED in tile-gold, DATED in tile-stone, each chip using its -bg and -fg pair together) plus a second 11px line carrying the single most important caveat in four words (o3 score self-reported, numbers from Dec 2024, ranks 1-2 tied, board frozen Feb 2026). DELIBERATELY no raw score on the row: a bare number needs a date, a baseline, and a caveat to mean anything, and the row cannot carry all three honestly, so it carries the STATUS word and the four-word caveat instead. The full set of ~13 reads as a complete, bounded, learnable field.

detailSurface.concept: A two-column split: prose left, leader panel right, divided by the 1px vertical rule. CRITICAL build correction from the critiques: use the .trend-detail-split / .trend-detail-rule class (collapses to one column and hides the rule at <=720px, which is correct), NOT .concept-shell (which HIDES its aside entirely at 1279px and would never show the rail on a 1040px surface). Also: the existing trend-detail page wraps at maxWidth 800px; for /benchmarks we set the page wrapper to --maxw-content (1040px) so the list and detail pages share width and the rail has room, then keep prose measure capped at ~720px inside the left column. Top of the left column, before any number: BackLink to /benchmarks (the digest component verbatim), then the domain kicker plus the STATUS word rendered large as the page tone-setter with a one-sentence plain gloss of what that status MEANS (NEAR CEILING: top models score so close together that the gaps are inside the measurement noise, so this test no longer tells you which model is better). Then the H1 name, then ONE bold lead sentence using the benchmark's own difficulty contrast as the definition (bus-stop test). Then the four beats, each a SectionEyebrow over prose, HairRule between, in fixed order: (1) WHAT IT MEASURES, plain definition plus a labeled EXAMPLE TASK in a left-rule-indented quiet block so difficulty is felt before any percent; (2) WHY YOU SHOULD CARE, the whyCare line, placed second; (3) HOW SCORING WORKS, the metric, the scale, who runs the board, and the calibration anchor (chance 25 percent, experts ~81 percent, prior SOTA X) so no figure appears naked; (4) WHAT TO WATCH, full structural parity, its SectionEyebrow in --color-gold and a 3px --color-gold ThinBar UNDER the eyebrow (horizontal, matching the real ThinBar geometry, NOT a vertical rail), where contamination, saturation, and the named controversy get a full plain-English paragraph plus an outbound read-the-disclosure editorial-link (FrontierMath funding plus the needsRecheck pre-correction warning; the public SWE-bench Verified deprecation). Catalog cross-links embed inline at first mention via editorial-link, and a closing To go deeper cluster lists the relatedConcepts as editorial-links into /concepts. RIGHT rail holds only the leader panel.
sections: STATUS gloss (large, plain-language meaning of the trust word)
Lead sentence (bus-stop definition via difficulty contrast)
WHAT IT MEASURES (+ indented Example task)
WHY YOU SHOULD CARE (placed second, before mechanics)
HOW SCORING WORKS (metric, scale, who runs it, calibration anchor)
WHAT TO WATCH (gold eyebrow + 3px gold ThinBar, full parity, controversy link)
To go deeper (inline + closing editorial-link cluster into /concepts)

leaderPanel.concept: A hairline-separated top-3 in the right rail, framed by a per-panel dated stamp on its own HairRule: a SectionEyebrow reading LEADERS AS OF JUN 10 2026 directly above the rows, so a screenshot of just the panel carries its own date. Each row is the existing rank-badge plus content split: left a soft tile-indigo ordinal (1, 2, 3), right the ScoreStamp atom, the welded unit grafted from Data-Forward. The ScoreStamp is three stacked lines that can never be separated: line 1, model name (15px weight 600) plus lab (12px text-2); line 2, the score taken VERBATIM from the data's free-text score string (so 1510 plus or minus 11 and 79.2% resolved (500-instance Verified split) both render as authored, preserving intervals and split-name caveats) plus, immediately beside it, a 12px text-2 baseline gloss (PhD experts ~81%, random chance 25%, top of 360+ models); line 3, the normalized as-of label plus source (Jun 2026, arena.ai). Only the date label is normalized to Mon YYYY; the verbatim asOfDate stays in a title attribute. Trust flags render INLINE, never on hover: selfReported true paints a small SELF-REPORTED chip in tile-steel; disputed true paints a DISPUTED chip in tile-gold; a near-tie (driven by a separate required boolean, NOT conflated with the CONTESTED status word, per the Data-Forward critique) prints a quiet near tie mark and a single spanning line under the three rows. Below the rows: STATUS as a labeled line, and when boardLastUpdated is null or older than the leaders' asOfDate, a muted board last updated MAR 2026, may be behind line (computed at render time from the hardcoded dates against the build date; there is no live syncedAt in static v1, so this is a date-vs-date comparison, not the Pulse Index cron-stale logic reused verbatim). HONEST-EMPTY STATE is first-class: where there is no trustworthy current top-3 (LiveCodeBench has no parseable board; several benchmarks only have historical anchors), the panel does NOT fabricate three rows. It shows No reliable current leaderboard. The most cited figure is a dated anchor: followed by the single dated anchor, never a blank or a zero. Ties (HLE, SWE-bench Verified have tied rank-1 entries in the data) render as two rank-1 rows sharing the (1) badge with a tied label, not a fake clean ordering. The cron later swaps the three strings and the stamp; zero structural change.
leaderPanel.treatment: Number-forward inside the rail only, never in the list. The score is the visual hero of each leader row via verbatim string plus tabular-nums, but it is always welded to its date and gloss. Color is disciplined: the rank badge is tile-indigo (recedes), caveat chips are tile-steel (self-reported) and tile-gold (disputed), and gold is the only caveat hue, always paired with a literal word so it survives grayscale and colorblindness. No heatmap, no green/red score gradient (the research is explicit that cross-benchmark scores are not comparable). A required trust enum plus a required near-tie boolean mean an unset state fails loud rather than rendering a falsely clean winner.

synthesis.motionAndAccent: Motion is the existing editorial vocabulary only, all CSS, all gated by usePrefersReducedMotion. List rows use the trendCellIn entrance with the capped ~18ms stagger. The benchmark name is the only hover-animated element per row via the editorial-link underline sweep plus arrow nudge (the ::after keyframe block copied by value from trends-client); no row background fill, no shadow, no scale, so rows read as index entries not buttons. Build note from the critiques: if a full-cell absolute Link is used as the click target, the name sweep must be triggered from a cell-level :hover via a descendant selector, not from hovering the name span, or the sweep will not fire. The detail page uses the trend-detail headline entrance. Accent discipline: --color-accent is #4255FF (Quizlet blue) via data-surface=editorial inside the (main) data-theme=light shell, used only for editorial-link underlines and the active CategoryTab sweep. Exactly one structural recolor on the whole surface: the WHAT TO WATCH SectionEyebrow and its 3px ThinBar swap accent for --color-gold (#B8860C text in light theme). Trust state is carried by the WORD first and gold or tile tint second, never color alone.
repoGrounding: app/globals.css: wrap pages in data-surface="editorial" + data-theme="light". Tokens: --maxw-content (1040px) for both list and detail width; --color-bg #FAF9F5 ground; --color-text / --color-text-2 #6B6B73 / --color-text-3 for size-and-opacity hierarchy; --text-xl (24px) for hero name, --text-lg (20px) and --text-md (16px) for leader model names, --color-border #E8E5DC for every hairline.

STATUS chip tokens (verified to exist; use -bg and -fg as a PAIR): LIVE = --tile-sage-bg #E2EED5 / --tile-sage-fg #4F7028; NEAR CEILING = --tile-steel-bg #D8DEEC / --tile-steel-fg #38486D (note: --tile-slate does NOT exist, the runners-up that named it were wrong); CONTESTED = --tile-gold-bg #FBE2A6 / --tile-gold-fg #855208; DATED = --tile-stone-bg #DDDDE3 / --tile-stone-fg #43434E; ordinal/rank badge = --tile-indigo-bg #DDDAFB / --tile-indigo-fg #4F40A0.

Caveat hue: --color-gold (#B8860C light) for the WHAT TO WATCH eyebrow and ThinBar; --color-gold-soft (#FBEFCE) only if a soft background tint is needed. These are distinct tokens, not interchangeable; chips need a bg and an fg.

components/trend-detail-client.tsx: ThinBar is a 3px HORIZONTAL bar (height: 3, position relative, fill via left-anchored width). Reuse it horizontally under the WHAT TO WATCH eyebrow recolored to --color-gold. Do NOT use it as a vertical left rail (that geometry does not exist). Also reuse the trend-detail headline entrance and the BackLink pattern.

globals.css .trend-detail-split + .trend-detail-rule: the correct two-column split, collapses to one column and hides the rule at <=720px. Use THIS, not .concept-shell (which hides its aside at 1279px). The existing trend-detail wrapper is maxWidth 800px; set the benchmarks wrapper to --maxw-content instead so list and detail share width.

components/trends-client.tsx: copy by value SectionEyebrow (11px uppercase 0.18em), the editorial-link sweep style block, CategoryTab (ghost-text 2px accent sweep, re-pointed to the TRUST filter axis), the 28px tabular tile ordinal badge from TrendCell, ArrowRight, and usePrefersReducedMotion + trendCellIn.

components/digest-client.tsx: copy by value HairRule, BackLink, the numbered-row grid rhythm, the Why it matters eyebrow-over-prose block (becomes WHY YOU SHOULD CARE), and the Related in the catalog inline editorial-link cluster (becomes the catalog cross-links).

docs/research/benchmarks-research-round2.json: the authoritative data. Per benchmark it has whatItMeasures, whyCare, scoring, watchOutFor, status, boardLastUpdated (nullable), needsRecheck, relatedConcepts, and leaders[] with score (FREE-TEXT string, keep verbatim, e.g. '1510 plus or minus 11' and '79.2% resolved (500-instance Verified split)'), asOfDate, selfReported, disputed. Known gaps to encode as honest-empty or DATED: LiveCodeBench (no parseable board), FrontierMath (needsRecheck, pre-correction), MMLU-Pro (board frozen 2026-03-11), SWE-bench Verified (tied self-reported rank-1 entries, public deprecation).

New work is small and additive: two route files under app/(main); a ScoreStamp component (3 swappable strings); a required status enum + a required nearTie boolean on the data model so unset fails loud; a status->chip-token map; a date-normalizer to 'Mon YYYY'; and a hand-authored one-line baseline gloss + four-word caveat per benchmark. Everything else is assembly of existing primitives. v1 is static; the future cron writes only score/model/asOf strings into the same components.
graftsFromRunnersUp: From Data-Forward: the ScoreStamp atom (score + as-of + trust flag welded into one inseparable typographic unit) so a screenshot always carries the date, and the cron later mutates only three strings per row with zero layout change.

From Data-Forward: make trust state a REQUIRED enum and add a SEPARATE required near-tie boolean (its critique correctly showed CONTESTED and near-tie are orthogonal: FrontierMath is contested with a clean ranking, arena.ai is a near-tie but not 'contested'). Unset fails loud rather than rendering a falsely clean winner.

From Magazine-Index: keep the leader score string VERBATIM and normalize only the date label, preserving intervals and split-name caveats authored in the data; stash the raw asOfDate in a title attribute.
From Magazine-Index: the curated-field-guide framing and the search affordance so a student arriving with a known name (SWE-bench, GPQA) is not forced to scan.

From Editorial Minimalist: single-column list to kill false cross-benchmark comparison; inline catalog cross-link at first mention of a concept; never let color carry meaning alone (gold always paired with a literal word).

From Editorial Minimalist: the stale-board second line on the dated stamp, but reimplemented as a static date-vs-date comparison (boardLastUpdated vs leaders' asOfDate vs build date), NOT the Pulse Index cron-stale logic, since static v1 has no syncedAt.
openQuestions: STATUS taxonomy: confirm the four words are LIVE / NEAR CEILING / CONTESTED / DATED. Several benchmarks are genuinely two states at once (FrontierMath is CONTESTED and approaching saturation; HumanEval is NEAR CEILING and somewhat DATED). We show the single dominant state on the list and let WHAT TO WATCH hold the nuance. James decides the dominant state per benchmark and who re-justifies it when leaders refresh.

Final v1 benchmark set and count. ~13 listed, but LiveCodeBench has no parseable leader data and would ship as honest-empty. Include it (teaches 'no trustworthy board exists') or drop it from v1? This also sets whether the single-column list has an awkward count.

Authoring volume reality. Each benchmark needs a hand-authored baseline gloss, a four-word caveat, an example task (some GPQA questions are licensed from the paper), and a WHAT TO WATCH paragraph with a real source link. This is closer to effort-L editorial work than effort-M. Confirm the editorial budget before build, or stage the launch (ship the 6-8 best-sourced benchmarks first).

Honest-empty volume. Only arena.ai has a verified current top-3; most others are historical anchors. That means most v1 panels show a DATED anchor or honest-empty state. Confirm this is acceptable as a deliberate stance and approve the exact copy, since the cumulative effect could read as an unfinished page to a student expecting live data.

Search vs filter scope for v1. Confirm both the TRUST CategoryTab filter and the free-text search box are in v1, or defer search to keep scope tight (risking the 'arrived with a known name, forced to scan' problem).

Cron source feasibility (informs v2, not v1 build). GPQA Diamond, FrontierMath, and ARC-AGI-2 have no canonical machine-readable board to pull from, so the future weekly refresh is hand-curation for those, not an automated swap. Flag now so v2 expectations are set.
